title: worker-report-v1: define schema and audit-label convention
description:
## Purpose

Define the pinned YAML contract that every worker agent (tdd-red-team, tdd-green-team, bug-diagnoser) returns to the implement-bead orchestrator, plus the file-based audit convention.

## Scope

Create docs/specs/worker-report-v1.md covering:

### Schema (YAML)

```yaml
status: complete | needs_human | failed
iteration: 1                       # optional; orchestrator-supplied for RALF-IT loops, omitted for single-shot dispatches
evidence:
  tests:
    command: "..."
    exit_code: 0
    passing: 42                    # required for green-loop convergence tracking
    failing: 0                     # required for green-loop convergence tracking
    output_excerpt: "..."
  build: { command: "...", exit_code: 0 }
  lint: { command: "...", exit_code: 0 }
  typecheck: { command: "...", exit_code: 0 }
gate_status: pass | fail | partial  # rolled-up gate verdict for orchestrator decision-making
root_cause_note: |                  # optional; required for bug-diagnoser, optional for others
  Free-text root-cause analysis. tdd-green-team in fix-bug receives this from the prior diagnose dispatch.
escalations:
  - reason: "one-line reason human needed"
    detail: "why this can't be resolved by the worker"
discovered_work:
  - title: "summary of found issue"
    type: bug | task | chore
    priority: 0..4
    detail: "optional context"
    # NOTE: no parent_hint or relation field. Orchestrator owns placement
    # per beads.md I3 (sibling test). Default is discovered-from edge to
    # the source bead.
commits:
  - sha: "full 40-char sha"
    message: "one-line commit message"
```

### File-based audit convention

- Orchestrator decides report filename: `<repo-root>/.beads/worker-reports/<step-bead-id>/<agent-name>[-iter<N>].yaml`
- For RALF-IT iterations, append iteration suffix: `<agent-name>-iter<N>.yaml`. Iteration counter is supplied by the formula step (which owns the loop), passed to the orchestrator, and embedded in the schema's `iteration` field.
- Path lives OUTSIDE any worktree so reports survive worktree cleanup.
- Orchestrator passes the target file path to the worker as task-spec input.
- Worker writes the YAML report to that file before exiting.
- Orchestrator reads the file after worker completes and stamps a label on the STEP-BEAD: `worker-report-<agent-name>[-iter<N>]` (boolean-style marker, dash-separated). Path is fully deterministic from the convention above; anyone with the step-bead id can find the file. Avoiding embedded paths in labels removes shell-quoting/charset friction.

### Audit-label policy

- Audit labels are FORENSIC-ONLY: append-only, not garbage-collected, not used for `bd ready --label` filtering.
- Iteration-suffixed labels (`...-iter<N>`) make the label set grow per RALF-IT iteration; this is intentional (forensic trail).
- If filterability is wanted later, a separate stable label (e.g. `worker-reported`) can be added as a sibling without disturbing the iteration-stamped ones.

### Malformed and crashed-worker handling

- If the worker exits non-zero or never writes the file, the orchestrator writes a SYNTHETIC worker-report with `status: failed`, `evidence.tests.exit_code` set to the worker's exit code, and `escalations` populated with the tail of stderr. The audit label is stamped anyway so the synthetic report is discoverable.
- If the file exists but is unparseable / missing required fields, same treatment: synthesize a `status: failed` report wrapping the parse error and the raw bytes.
- Required fields the orchestrator MUST be able to read: `status`, `gate_status`. Missing either ⇒ malformed.

### Documentation only

Documented YAML, no JSON-schema enforcement (matches existing spec style).

## Acceptance Criteria

- docs/specs/worker-report-v1.md exists
- Schema example matches structure above (includes iteration, gate_status, test counts, root_cause_note)
- Audit convention covers: file path format, RALF-IT iteration handling, label format, audit-label policy (forensic-only), malformed-and-crashed handling
- Doc explicitly notes that discovered-work placement (parent vs discovered-from) is decided by the orchestrator, not the worker
- Build passes. Typecheck passes. Tests pass.
close_reason: worker-report-v1 spec set delivered: shared core + tdd-red/green + bug-diagnoser per-agent extensions; merged via PR #29 (de0c181)

title: Redesign worker agent layer: split bead-implementor, define worker-report-v1, strip orchestrator concerns
description:
## Summary

Epic: redesign the worker agent layer of the bead pipeline. Replace the bead-aware mega-agent (`bead-implementor`) with three focused, bead-agnostic worker agents and a pinned, file-based, audit-friendly report contract (`worker-report-v1`).

## Background / Motivation

PR #28 implemented the per-stage `claude -p` pipeline (bead 7bk.9). Post-review, the `bead-implementor` agent design was found to have four structural defects that must be corrected before further work on the pipeline.

### Architectural defects being fixed

- **Defect A — Leaky abstraction.** The worker agent runs `bd label add`, `bd update --append-notes`, reads bead state directly. Workers should be pure task functions with no bead knowledge. The orchestrator (`implement-bead` skill) owns all bead state transitions.
- **Defect B — Mega-agent with stage branching.** One agent file has three `Stage Behaviors` sections (diagnose / red-tests / green-loop). The agent must conditionally ignore behavior based on a stage parameter. This is three agents pretending to be one. Separate agents have focused contexts, independent model/effort tuning, and no conditional noise.
- **Defect C — No structured report contract.** Workers are told "report back what you did" but there is no pinned schema. The orchestrator cannot reliably parse: completion status, evidence, escalations, discovered work, commits.
- **Defect D — Skill and command files are textbooks.** `implement-bead/SKILL.md` and `commands/implement-bead.md` contain mode-detection lore, driver references, invocation context. These are irrelevant to the operator; they should contain only imperatives.

The `bead-implementor` name also conflicts with the `implement-bead` skill/command. Replacement agents use role-focused names (`tdd-red-team`, `tdd-green-team`, `bug-diagnoser`).

## Requirements

The epic is delivered when all five child beads (7bk.19.1 through 7bk.19.5) are closed AND the cross-cutting integration assertion below holds.

### Decomposition (children)

1. **7bk.19.1 — `worker-report-v1` schema + audit-label convention.** Define `docs/specs/worker-report-v1.md`. Foundational contract.
2. **7bk.19.2 — Three worker agents.** Create `tdd-red-team`, `tdd-green-team`, `bug-diagnoser` under `src/plugins/beads/.agents/agents/`. Bead-agnostic, pure task functions, least-privilege tools whitelist, Write-tool (absolute path) for reports.
3. **7bk.19.3 — Orchestrator rewrite.** Rewrite `implement-bead/SKILL.md` (imperative-only, advisory ≤80 lines / hard fail at 150) and `commands/implement-bead.md` (≤15 lines). Receives explicit stage argument; per-dispatch only (does NOT own RALF-IT loop). Dispatch new agents, parse worker-report-v1 file, stamp audit label, synthesize report on crashed/malformed cases, apply discovered-from edge for filed work.
4. **7bk.19.4 — Formula wiring.** Update `implement-feature.formula.toml` and `fix-bug.formula.toml` to dispatch `implement-bead` skill with explicit stage and iteration arguments. Loop-bearing steps (green-loop, fix) include explicit MAX_ITERATIONS, convergence predicate, and max-iterations-exhausted escalation in their prose.
5. **7bk.19.5 — Cleanup.** Delete `bead-implementor.md`, update `docs/specs/bead-pipeline-architecture.md` (agent catalog + stage execution + per-dispatch boundary note), update `scripts/smoke/verify-artifacts.sh` to check the three new agents, add an orchestrator-contract unit test (parse + stamp against good and malformed fixtures), and add a sandbox tracer-bullet for outside-worktree writes.

### Dependency DAG

```
A (.1) ──┬─> B (.2) ─┬─> C (.3) ─┬─> D (.4) ─> E (.5)
         │           │           │
         ├───────────┘           └───────────────^
         │
         └────────> D (.4)        # explicit edge: 19.1 path convention is load-bearing for 19.4
```

A blocks B, C, AND D. B blocks C and D. C blocks D and E. D blocks E.

## Out of Scope

- No changes to the higher-level molecule lifecycle (claim walk, close walk, squash semantics) — those remain as defined.
- No changes to RALF-IT iteration *count* / convergence policy beyond the ownership-boundary change (formula step owns the loop, orchestrator is per-dispatch).
- No re-introduction of variable-effort policy from 7bk.15 — flat `effort: high` for all three agents (per Q3). May be filed as a separate optimization bead.
- **Orchestrator self-crash recovery.** The synthetic-report fallback covers worker crashes. If the orchestrator itself crashes mid-dispatch (after worker exit but before label-stamp / report-parse completes), the step-bead is left in a 'worker ran but no audit label' limbo. Detecting and recovering from this case is OUT OF SCOPE for this epic; file a follow-up if it becomes a real-world problem.
- No changes to `bd` itself.
- No new agent types beyond the three named here.

## Design Notes (decisions made during brainstorming + 2 adversarial review passes)

### Q1 — Worker output mechanism: file-based, audit-trailed

Workers do NOT inline-print their report. The orchestrator computes a target file path, passes it as part of the worker's task spec, and the worker writes the YAML report to that path before exiting. The orchestrator reads the file after the worker exits.

Rationale: auditability. Files survive worktree cleanup and can be inspected long after the run.

**File-path convention:** `<repo-root>/.beads/worker-reports/<step-bead-id>/<agent-name>[-iter<N>].yaml` (outside any worktree; `-iter<N>` suffix used for RALF-IT iterations).

**Audit label** (revised after pass-1): `worker-report-<agent-name>[-iter<N>]` — boolean-style marker, dash-separated. Path is fully deterministic from the convention above; embedding the path in the label was rejected to avoid shell-quoting/charset friction. Stamped on the STEP-bead, not the source bead.

**Audit-label policy** (added after pass-2): forensic-only, append-only, NOT garbage-collected, NOT used for `bd ready --label` filtering. Iteration-suffixed labels grow per RALF-IT iteration intentionally.

**Crashed/malformed handling** (added after pass-1): if the worker exits non-zero, never writes the file, or writes an unparseable file, the orchestrator synthesizes a `status: failed` report capturing the worker's exit code and stderr tail (or parse error). The audit label is stamped anyway so the synthetic report is discoverable.

**Sandbox writability** (added after pass-2 risk): workers MUST use the `Write` tool with an absolute path, NOT Bash redirection, because `claude -p` subagents inside a worktree may have CWD/sandbox constraints that block Bash redirection outside the worktree. A tracer-bullet in the 19.5 smoke confirms this contract works in practice.

### Q2 — Formula wiring is in scope (this epic)

Without updating `implement-feature.formula.toml` and `fix-bug.formula.toml` to dispatch the new agents, the new agents exist but no caller dispatches them. Wiring is mechanical but architecturally load-bearing — kept inside the epic as child 7bk.19.4.

### Q3 — Effort policy: flat `high` for all three agents

Current spec for `bead-implementor` had variable effort (high for iter 1, medium for iter 2-N) per 7bk.15. New design collapses to flat `effort: high` on all three agents.

Rationale: simplicity now; the existing variable-effort mention in arch spec must also be updated (handled in child 7bk.19.5). Re-introducing variable effort can be filed as a separate optimization bead later if cost becomes an issue.

### Per-dispatch / iteration-loop boundary (added after pass-1)

The orchestrator skill (`implement-bead`) is the PER-DISPATCH primitive: given a step-bead and an explicit stage argument, it dispatches one worker once and processes one report. The orchestrator does NOT own the RALF-IT iteration loop; the formula step does.

**Loop ownership in TOML prose** (tightened after pass-2): formula step bodies are LLM-executed natural language with no mechanical loop construct. To prevent rediscovering Defect B inside the prose, every loop-bearing step MUST explicitly state in its body: `MAX_ITERATIONS=<N>` (literal), the convergence predicate (against worker-report-v1 fields), and the max-iterations-exhausted escalation behavior.

The stage argument is passed EXPLICITLY by the formula step, not derived heuristically from the step-bead title or description.

### Discovered-work placement (added after pass-1)

Workers do NOT decide parent-vs-discovered-from placement for entries in `discovered_work`. The schema deliberately omits a `relation` or `parent_hint` field. The orchestrator applies the default `discovered-from` edge to the source bead, per beads.md I3.

### Tools whitelist (added after pass-1)

All three worker agents pin the same tools whitelist as the current `bead-implementor` (`Read, Edit, Write, Grep, Glob, Bash`) for least privilege. Inheriting all configured tools (browser MCP, web fetch, etc.) was rejected.

### Smoke verification: real, not symbolic (tightened after pass-2)

The 19.5 smoke MUST exercise actual orchestrator code paths against good and malformed fixtures. The earlier 'static-only relaxation' (presence of an example file) was REJECTED because it would defeat the entire purpose of the smoke — proving fixture file existence proves nothing about wiring.

### Decide-myself items (no question to user, recorded for transparency)

- **Schema enforcement:** documented YAML in `docs/specs/worker-report-v1.md`. No JSON-schema enforcement (matches existing spec style in this repo).
- **Smoke implementation form:** unit-level test inside or alongside `scripts/smoke/verify-artifacts.sh`, form open (shell function, separate test file, or sub-process invocation).
- **Grep exclusion:** the final invariant grep excludes `.beads/issues.jsonl` because the issue tracker stores this epic's own spec text (which mentions the thing being replaced).

## Acceptance Criteria

- All five child beads (7bk.19.1, 7bk.19.2, 7bk.19.3, 7bk.19.4, 7bk.19.5) are closed.
- After all children close, the invariant grep `grep -rn 'bead-implementor' --include='*.md' --include='*.toml' --include='*.sh' . | grep -v '.claude/worktrees/' | grep -v '.git/' | grep -v '.beads/issues.jsonl'` returns zero matches.
- `scripts/smoke/verify-artifacts.sh` passes end-to-end against the new agent set, including the orchestrator-contract unit test (parse + stamp on good and malformed fixtures) and the sandbox tracer-bullet.
- Build passes. Typecheck passes. Tests pass.
acceptance_criteria: All five child beads (7bk.19.1 through 7bk.19.5) closed.
notes: Design rationale: The current bead-implementor.md on main is architecturally flawed — it is bead-aware (runs bd commands directly), branches behavior across three stage roles in one file, and has no structured report contract. This bead exists to fix all three. The 'bead-implementor' name also conflicts with implement-bead skill/command; the replacement agents use role-focused names (tdd-red-team, tdd-green-team, bug-diagnoser). Discovered during 7bk.9 PR review (PR #28).

title: install.sh: --prune 'all' selection clobbers PATH via zsh `path` tied-array, breaks backup()
notes:
Reproduction: ./scripts/install.sh --prune-only, then 'a' at the prompt under zsh on macOS.

Symptom: 'backup:5: command not found: date' (and presumably also 'rm', but error short-circuits at date first).

Root cause: zsh has a special variable `path` (lowercase) that is a tied array to `PATH` (uppercase). Assigning to local `path` reassigns `PATH` to the orphan path string for the duration of the calling function. _delete_orphan does `local tool=\"$1\" path=\"$2\"`, calls backup() which calls `date` — `date` not found because PATH is now `/Users/scott/.claude/skills/resolve-pr-comments` (the first orphan).

Affected lines in scripts/install.sh:
- 1084: _display_orphans local declaration includes 'path' (cosmetically wrong, may not crash since loop body uses only printf builtin)
- 1109: _delete_orphan local — THE BUG that breaks 'a' selection
- 1183-1189: per-item interactive loop ('o' selection) — same bug; would crash on the first y/Y selection too

Fix: rename 'path' to a safe name in all three sites. 'orphan_path' is clearest; 'p' is shortest. Affects local declarations and references inside those functions.

Verification: under expect with PTY, run `./scripts/install.sh --prune-only` then send 'a' — should complete without 'command not found' errors. Same with 'o' followed by 'y'.

Severity: P1 — feature is broken on macOS where zsh re-exec is mandatory (system /bin/sh is bash 3.2, so the script ALWAYS re-execs to zsh on macOS).
close_reason: Fixed in PR #27 (squash-merged as 53d7ed2). Renamed local 'path' to 'orphan_path' in three sites in scripts/install.sh: _delete_orphan, _display_orphans, and per-item interactive loop. Added warning comments at all three sites referencing the zsh tied-array gotcha.

title: resolve-human-bead skill + HEP rollout
description:
A new skill `resolve-human-bead`, plus the cross-skill rollout of the **Human-Escalation Pattern (HEP)** defined in `docs/specs/bead-pipeline-architecture.md` §5.6 and `src/plugins/beads/.claude/rules/beads.md`.

## Background / Motivation

Under the prior architecture, agent stages stamped `human` on both the step-bead and the source bead when blocked. This was structurally broken: the `human` label is a *visibility tag* on `bd human list`, **not** a gate on `bd ready`. A `human`-labeled `implementation-ready` source bead remained visible to `bd ready --label implementation-ready`, allowing a separate agent to grab paused work.

HEP fixes this by modeling the pause as a `bd dep` blocking edge to a freshly-created escalation bead. `bd ready`'s blocker-aware filtering then naturally excludes the source bead, and the escalation bead becomes the unit of human attention. See `docs/specs/bead-pipeline-architecture.md` §5.6 for the full protocol; `src/plugins/beads/.claude/rules/beads.md` carries the operational summary every agent needs.

## Scope (work owned by THIS bead)

1. **New skill** at `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/resolve-human-bead/SKILL.md`. Detects the bead's class, reads context (notes, comments, source bead state, molecule, worktree), interactively diagnoses the resume scenario, and applies the right primitive with user confirmation.

   **Class detection** (in priority order — first match wins):
   - **Merge-gate hand-off sub-class** (HEP escalation, but resolution routes through merge-and-cleanup) — bead has `human` label AND `merge-ready` label AND title prefix `[Merge gate]`. Created by `merge-or-handoff`'s hand-off path (arch §3, §5.6). Resolution: Scenario G (route to `/merge-and-cleanup`).
   - **`[h]` follow-up** (arch §4.3) — bead has `human` label AND has `parent` set AND title prefix `[Human verify]`. Resolution: scenario F.
   - **HEP escalation** — bead has `human` label AND has at least one incoming `bd dep` from a non-closed source bead AND lacks the discriminators above. Resolution: scenarios A–E.
   - **Orphan human task** — bead has `human` label, no incoming `bd dep`, no `[h]` or `[Merge gate]` shape. Resolution: prompt user; treat as standalone task (likely scenario E or scenario-specific close per user's intent).
   - **Inconsistent state (Repair branch — distinct from Scenario G/G-merge above)** — (a) escalation is closed but a non-closed source still has an open dep edge pointing at it (someone bare-`bd close`d the escalation); OR (b) source is closed while the escalation is still open (orphaned escalation). Surface the inconsistency to the user; do NOT silently auto-resolve. Offer concrete repair actions (close orphaned escalation; remove stale dep edge).
   - **Source-bead pivot** — user invoked `/resolve-human-bead <source-id>` on a source bead that itself has no `human` label but has an open `human`-labeled blocker. Skill detects via the canonical probe below and pivots to the blocker. If multiple `human`-labeled blockers exist, list them with one-line context for each and prompt the user to pick one; resolve one per invocation.

   Class-detection priority is **important**: the merge-gate sub-class probe MUST run before generic HEP-escalation detection, because a `[Merge gate]`-titled bead also has `human` label + incoming dep (it satisfies the HEP-escalation predicate too), and routing it through scenarios A–E would be wrong (the resolution is merge-and-cleanup, not `bd human respond`).

   **Canonical detection probe** (source → `human`-labeled blocker pivot):
   ```bash
   # List source's open dep blockers, then check each for human label
   bd show <source-id> --json \
     | jq -r '.[0].dependencies[]? | select(.type=="blocks") | .issue_id' \
     | while read blocker; do
         BSTATE=$(bd show "$blocker" --json | jq -r '.[0].status')
         [ "$BSTATE" = "closed" ] && continue
         bd label list "$blocker" --json | jq -e 'index("human")' >/dev/null && echo "$blocker"
       done
   ```
   The implementer MUST verify `bd show --json`'s `dependencies[]` field shape against the live schema before shipping (the project memory `bd-cli-quirks` notes jq paths for dep objects can trip people up); document the verified jq path inline in the SKILL.md.

   **Seven scenario branches** — each with its own resolution primitive:
   - **A. Spec amended** (HEP escalation class) → user edits source bead's description (manually or via skill-prompted text editor); skill closes via `bd human respond <human-id> --response "<one-line summary of amendment>"`.
   - **B. Scope expanded** (HEP escalation class) → skill prompts user for new sibling bead's title/description; runs `bd create ... --type task --priority "<inherit>"` + `bd dep add <source-id> <new-id>` BEFORE `bd human respond <human-id> --response "<scope expansion noted, dep added>"`.
   - **C. Tooling/credential resolved** (HEP escalation class) → ONLY `bd human respond <human-id> --response "<what was resolved>"`. No other state changes.
   - **D. Architectural rework** (HEP escalation class) → `bd mol squash <mol-id> --summary "<rework summary>"` (with user confirmation — destructive); user edits source description; `bd human respond <human-id> --response "<rework noted, molecule reset>"`.
   - **E. Abandoned** (HEP escalation class) → `bd human dismiss <human-id> --reason "<why abandoned>"` (with user confirmation) + `bd close <source-id> --reason "<why source abandoned>"` (with user confirmation, separate prompt). Skill MUST NOT invoke `bd human respond`.
   - **F. `[h]` follow-up verified** (`[h]` follow-up class only) → `bd label add <follow-up-id> verified-by-human` + plain `bd close <follow-up-id> --reason "<verification summary>"` (NOT `bd human respond` — close-walk semantics matter; arch §4.3 enforces verification via the merge-and-cleanup gate-step's `verified-by-human` check).
   - **G. Merge-gate cleared** (merge-gate hand-off sub-class only) → invoke `/merge-and-cleanup <source-id>` (which merges the PR, runs cleanup, closes the hand-off escalation bead AND the `merge-{source-id}` close-gate child as final actions). Skill MUST NOT invoke `bd human respond` or `bd human dismiss` on the hand-off escalation bead — `/merge-and-cleanup` owns its closure as part of the merge transaction. If `/merge-and-cleanup` is unavailable, surface the manual fallback (the user runs the merge themselves, then closes the escalation via `bd human respond <human-id> --response "Merged manually"` once the merge action completed).

   **Destructive actions requiring explicit user confirmation:** `bd mol squash <mol-id>` (D), `bd human dismiss <human-id>` (E), `bd close <source-id>` (E). Confirmation prompt MUST quote the exact id and reason about to be applied. (Scenario G's destructive actions — PR merge, branch deletion, worktree teardown — are owned by `/merge-and-cleanup`, which has its own confirmation contract; `resolve-human-bead`'s only action in G is to invoke `/merge-and-cleanup`.)

   **Prohibited primitives:** bare `bd label remove <id> human` (bypasses audit trail); bare `bd close <id>` on any `human`-labeled bead (bypasses audit trail). Both are explicitly listed in the SKILL.md "Red Flags" section.

2. **Slash-command wrapper** at `src/plugins/beads/.claude/commands/resolve-human-bead.md`. Thin wrapper taking `$ARGUMENTS` (the bead id — escalation, follow-up, or source-with-blocker). Behavior when `$ARGUMENTS` has no `human` label and no `human`-labeled blocker: print a one-line message (`"No human escalation found on <id>; nothing to resolve."`) and exit cleanly (zero exit). Do NOT escalate, do NOT auto-create a human bead.

3. **start-bead Route D** at `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/start-bead/SKILL.md`. Add Route D between Step 2 (molecule probe) and Step 3 (Routes A/B/C):
   - **Trigger** — target bead has `human` label OR has at least one open `bd dep` blocker carrying `human`.
   - **Action** — Route D **AUTO-INVOKES** the `resolve-human-bead` skill in the current session via the Skill tool, passing the appropriate target id (the bead itself if `human`-labeled, else the `human`-labeled blocker; if multiple blockers, defer the pick to the skill). The user sees ONE continuous flow; no second `/resolve-human-bead` invocation is required.
   - **Surface message** — print one line before invocation: `"Route D: <id> [is | is blocked by] human-labeled <target>. Invoking resolve-human-bead..."`.

4. **implement-bead HEP rollout** at `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/implement-bead/SKILL.md`. Replace every "stamp `human` on BOTH step-bead AND source bead" with the HEP escalation procedure (create escalation bead + label `human` + structured notes + `bd dep add <source> <human>` + revert source `in_progress → open`). Affected paths include: missing/invalid worktree label, ambiguous formula label, multiple-molecules disambiguation failure, missing or unparseable upstream report (diagnose → red-tests), worker `needs_human` outcome, worker `failed` outcome, and the formula-fallback `epic` case.

5. **run-queue HEP cleanup** at `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/run-queue/SKILL.md`. Drop the "remove `human` label" advice (incorrect under HEP). Replace the spec-gap escalation procedure (currently `bd label add <bead-id> human`) with the HEP procedure. Keep the `bd human list` triage gate as the first thing run-queue checks each loop iteration — escalation beads still surface there.

6. **Documentation alignment** — architecture doc §5.6 and `src/plugins/beads/.claude/rules/beads.md` HEP section have already been rewritten as part of the same pivot transaction. Skill text MUST cite these as authority.

7. **Smoke tests** — two procedural smoke tests, run by hand against scratch fixtures, documented in PR description:

   - **Smoke 1 (HEP escalation, scenario C round-trip)** — explicit fixture recipe:
     1. In a scratch directory `mkdir -p /tmp/nhep-smoke-1 && cd /tmp/nhep-smoke-1`, run `bd init` and create a source bead: `B1=$(bd create --title "Test source" --type task --json | jq -r .id)`.
     2. Manually invoke the HEP escalation block from arch §5.6 with `Scenario hint: tooling-credentials`. (No formula needed; copy/paste the snippet substituting `$B1` and a synthetic context.)
     3. Verify (record in PR): `bd show $B1` shows status `open` and `dependencies` contains the new escalation `$H1`; `bd ready --json | jq '[.[] | select(.id==$B1)] | length'` returns 0; `bd label list $B1` does NOT contain `human`.
     4. Run `/resolve-human-bead $H1`. Skill prompts class → HEP escalation; scenario → C; runs `bd human respond $H1`.
     5. Verify: `bd show $H1` → closed with reason `Responded`; `bd ready` returns `$B1` (newly unblocked).
     **Expected outcome:** zero `human` labels on source bead at any point; round-trip succeeds; PR description records each verification step's actual output.

   - **Smoke 2 (`[h]` follow-up verification, scenario F)** — synthetic-fixture protocol with explicit limitation:
     1. **Limitation noted in PR description**: `[h]` follow-up beads are created exclusively by `brainstorm-bead.finalize` (arch doc §4.3), owned by bead **7bk.12** (still open / not implemented at 7bk.13's ship time). Until 7bk.12 lands, Smoke 2 verifies *only* the SKILL'S CLASS DETECTION + SCENARIO F PRIMITIVE CHOICE — it does NOT verify integration with real finalize-step output (e.g., the `merge-{source-id}` sibling bead behavior, multi-follow-up close-walk gating). Real-finalize integration testing tracked as discovered-work bead filed at 7bk.12 implementation time.
     2. Hand-craft a follow-up bead that mimics §4.3 shape: `S=$(bd create --title "Test source" --type task --json | jq -r .id); FU=$(bd create --type task --title "[Human verify] Test verification AC" --parent "$S" --json | jq -r .id); bd label add $FU human`.
     3. Run `/resolve-human-bead $FU`. Skill detects `[h]` class via title prefix + parent + `human` label. Prompts user for `verified-by-human` application. Runs `bd label add $FU verified-by-human` then plain `bd close $FU`.
     4. Verify: skill did NOT invoke `bd human respond` or `bd human dismiss`; `bd label list $FU` includes `verified-by-human`; `$FU` is closed; `$S` is unaffected (no I2 cascade because no other simulated children).
     **Expected outcome:** correct class detection; correct primitive choice (`verified-by-human` + plain `bd close`).

## Out of scope

- Changes to the `bd` CLI itself — HEP uses existing primitives (`bd create`, `bd label add`, `bd dep add`, `bd update --status`, `bd human respond`, `bd human dismiss`, `bd mol squash`).
- The `merge-or-handoff` hand-off path's HEP retrofit at the FORMULA-STEP TOML level. Architecture doc §5.6 describes the design (escalation bead with `merge-ready` label; merge step closes it). Whether the relevant formula `.toml` already encodes this is a separate concern; if implementation reveals a mismatch, file a discovered-work bead at that time (do not rewrite the formula as part of this bead).
- The b416 enforcement work — superseded by HEP's structural invariant (b416 closed as superseded as part of this pivot).
- The ffxh `bd close` audit on `human`-labeled beads — separate bead, narrowed scope post-pivot.
- Real-world `[h]` follow-up integration testing — deferred to 7bk.12 (which creates `[h]` follow-ups) plus a follow-up smoke-test bead filed at that time.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/resolve-human-bead/SKILL.md` exists with the six class-detection branches (merge-gate hand-off sub-class, `[h]` follow-up, HEP escalation, orphan, inconsistent-state repair branch, source-bead pivot) in the documented priority order, the canonical detection probe with verified jq paths inline, the destructive-action enumeration, and the prohibited-primitives section ("Red Flags": bare `bd label remove human`, bare `bd close <human-id>`).
- `src/plugins/beads/.claude/commands/resolve-human-bead.md` exists; passes `$ARGUMENTS` to the skill; on a target with no human-related state, prints one-line message and exits cleanly with zero status.
- **Per-scenario behavioral AC** (each independently testable):
  - **A**: invokes `bd human respond <human-id>`; description-edit step recorded BEFORE the close.
  - **B**: invokes `bd create` + `bd dep add <source> <new>` BEFORE `bd human respond <human-id>`.
  - **C**: invokes ONLY `bd human respond <human-id>`; no other state changes.
  - **D**: invokes `bd mol squash <mol-id>` (with prompted user confirmation) BEFORE `bd human respond <human-id>`.
  - **E**: invokes `bd human dismiss <human-id>` (with confirmation) AND `bd close <source-id>` (with separate confirmation); does NOT invoke `bd human respond`.
  - **F**: invokes `bd label add <follow-up> verified-by-human` AND plain `bd close <follow-up-id>`; does NOT invoke `bd human respond` or `bd human dismiss`.
  - **G**: invokes `/merge-and-cleanup <source-id>` (or surfaces manual fallback if unavailable); does NOT invoke `bd human respond` or `bd human dismiss` on the hand-off escalation directly.
- Multi-blocker handling: when source has >1 open `human`-labeled blocker, skill lists them all with title + status + age, prompts user to pick one, resolves one per invocation.
- Inconsistent-state class (G): skill detects (i) escalation closed with source still showing dep, (ii) source closed with escalation still open; surfaces inconsistency without auto-resolving; offers concrete repair actions.
- start-bead Route D **AUTO-INVOKES** `resolve-human-bead` in the same session via the Skill tool; user sees one continuous flow, no second invocation. Trigger covers both bead-itself-`human` and bead-blocked-by-`human` (and by extension, the merge-gate sub-class — Route D dispatches to the skill, which then routes to scenario G).
- implement-bead no longer stamps `human` on source/step-beads; all flag-human paths execute the HEP escalation procedure per `beads.md` HEP section and arch doc §5.6.
- run-queue no longer advises bare label removal; spec-gap escalation uses the HEP procedure.
- All HEP-affected skill files cite `beads.md` HEP section and arch doc §5.6 as authoritative.
- Smoke 1 (HEP scenario C round-trip) executed; PR description records the verifications from steps 3 and 5 with actual output snippets.
- Smoke 2 (`[h]` follow-up scenario F, synthetic fixture) executed; PR description records the limitation, the hand-crafted fixture commands, and the verifications.

## Dependencies

- (preceded by, in this same pivot transaction) Architecture doc §5.6 and `beads.md` rule rewritten to HEP.
- (supersedes) b416 — pause-gate detection no longer needed under HEP.
- (loosely related) ffxh — separate `bd close` audit, post-HEP scope.
- (parent epic) 7bk.20.
- (deferred-to) 7bk.12 — real-finalize integration testing for `[h]` follow-ups (see Out of scope).

Smoke 1 (HEP scenario C round-trip) executed; PR description records actual output of steps 3 and 5.
Smoke 2 ([h] follow-up scenario F, synthetic fixture) executed; PR description records limitation, hand-crafted fixture commands, and verifications.
notes:
## Open questions / minor risks (non-blocking)

Recorded after ralf-spec-review cycle 2 of 2 (formula cap reached). All cycle-1 findings (2 critical / 4 major / 2 minor / 1 nit) fixed; cycle 2 verdict READY-TO-FINALIZE. Three minor flags surfaced in cycle 2 are non-blocking and recorded here for the implementer:

1. **Smoke 1 escalation snippet (executability)** — Smoke 1 step 2 says 'copy/paste the snippet from arch §5.6 substituting $B1 and a synthetic context'. Arch §5.6 contains a runnable bash block authored alongside this bead (verified by the spec author at write time), but if §5.6 is later restructured to prose, Smoke 1 must inline its own snippet. Implementer should re-verify §5.6 contains a runnable bash block before running Smoke 1; if not, compose one from the primitives listed in scope item 4: `bd create --type task --title 'Human input needed: ...' --priority <inherit>`; `bd label add $H1 human`; `bd update $H1 --append-notes ...`; `bd dep add $B1 $H1`; `bd update $B1 --status open`.

2. **Canonical detection probe field name** — the probe uses `select(.type=="blocks")`. The reviewer flagged that 'blocks' may live on the blocker side (outgoing) while what shows on the source's `dependencies[]` may be 'blocked-by' (incoming). The spec correctly defers verification to the implementer ('MUST verify bd show --json's dependencies[] field shape against the live schema before shipping'); implementer must NOT skip that step. Memory `bd_cli_quirks` notes jq paths for dep objects trip people up.

3. **Class G interactive flow detail** — Class G repair actions are listed as bullet examples; per-case prompt structure not specced. Implementer should pattern-match scenarios A–F's confirmation-prompt style; class G is rare enough that this is acceptable as an implementer-time judgment call.

title: Create simplify skill (port from /simplify command body)
description:
Create a portable `simplify` skill at `src/user/.agents/skills/simplify/SKILL.md`, ported from the existing `/simplify` slash command body. **The skill name MUST be exactly `simplify`** — this is a hard constraint, not a brainstorm choice. Downstream sweep bead agents-config-7bk.8 literal-references the name `simplify` in 25+ dispatch sites; renaming would break them.

Goal: a single canonical invocation site ("use the simplify skill") that works for orchestrator, subagent, and foreign-agent contexts — eliminating the orchestrator-tier-only guard required by slash-command-based dispatch.

## Why a skill instead of fixing the slash command in place

- Slash commands can only be invoked by the orchestrator (the user-driven session). Subagents cannot fire them.
- `/simplify` lives outside this repo (sidekick/plugin injection — see agents-config-7bk.7 NOTES). Not portable to non-sidekick installs or Codex/Gemini.
- A skill living in `src/user/.agents/skills/` ships via `install.sh` to all three tools.
- Skill body can detect tool capability: parallel Agent dispatch when available, serial in-context execution as fallback.

## Sketch of intended scope (refine during brainstorm)

- Port the body of `/simplify` into `src/user/.agents/skills/simplify/SKILL.md` (name LOCKED — see top of this description)
- Modify Phase 2 to do tool-capability detection — "if Agent tool available, dispatch the three reviewers (reuse, quality, efficiency) concurrently; otherwise execute the three reviews in series in the current context"
- Decide single-source-of-truth strategy for /simplify command vs simplify skill (drift mitigation): comment-header sync directive, OR rewrite slash command body to invoke the skill, OR accept periodic re-sync as known cost
- Preserve everything currently encoded in the broken `code-simplifier` agent body that's worth keeping (worktree scoping, behavior-preserving discipline, project-memory persistence, boundary rules) — already inventoried in agents-config-7bk.7 NOTES

## Coordination

- Blocks: agents-config-7bk.8 (which sweeps stale dispatches across the codebase to use the new skill name)
- Adjacent: agents-config-7bk.7 (deletes the broken `code-simplifier` agent file; will adopt the skill form for completion-gate)
- 7bk.7 should re-evaluate its current decision (orchestrator-fires-/simplify) once this skill exists — likely flip to invoking the skill instead

## Out of scope for this bead (defer to consumers)

- Renaming dispatch sites in ralf-it, completion-gate, test-review, wait-for-pr-comments, reply-and-resolve-pr-threads, quality-reviewer, plugin formula TOMLs — that's 7bk.8's job
- Deleting the broken `code-simplifier` agent file — that's 7bk.7's job
acceptance_criteria:
1. src/user/.agents/skills/simplify/SKILL.md exists with `name: simplify` frontmatter and a three-phase body (identify changes → three-axis review → aggregate and fix), retaining the extracted Phase-2 reviewer checklist verbatim.
2. The skill body adds a new `Operating Discipline` section (placed between the intro and Phase 1, or equivalent placement) that integrates the four R4 disciplines into the existing extracted body. Implementer must NOT declare the body "already done" without adding this section.
3. Phase 2 explicitly handles both runtime shapes using capability-shaped wording (NOT tool-named): "If your runtime supports concurrent subagent dispatch in a single turn (Claude `Agent`, Codex `Task`, Gemini equivalents), dispatch the three reviewers in parallel; otherwise execute the three reviews serially in this context."
4. The Operating Discipline section preserves the apply-categories list (renames, dead-code removal, trivially-redundant-branch collapse, clearly-duplicated helper extraction) and flag-categories list (changes touching return values, side effects, error paths, exported APIs, or runtime ordering) — verbatim or with equivalent enumeration. Also includes worktree scoping, "no abstractions without ≥2 call sites", boundary rules (no scope creep, no test rewrites, no new deps, no commits/pushes), and host-agnostic memory guidance.
5. Memory guidance has an explicit no-memory fallback: "If the host has no persistent memory mechanism, surface the recurring pattern in the final summary under a `Recurring pattern observed:` line so the user can record it manually."
6. The skill file's first lines contain an HTML comment header recording: source = `/simplify` slash command (sidekick injection); drift policy = (C) accept periodic re-sync; last sync = 2026-05-02. Comment is placed BEFORE the `---` frontmatter so it does not corrupt YAML parsing, OR inside the markdown body above the H1, whichever the host parsers tolerate (verify by inspection of one existing skill).
7. `scripts/install.sh --dry-run --tools=claude,codex,gemini --plugins=beads` shows the skill stages into all three CLI destinations with no fatal collision.
8. No files outside `src/user/.agents/skills/simplify/` are modified by this bead (7bk.7/7bk.8 own the rest).
9. Build passes. Typecheck passes. Tests pass.
notes:
# Spec — Create simplify skill (port from /simplify command body)

## Summary

Create a portable `simplify` skill at `src/user/.agents/skills/simplify/SKILL.md` ported from the existing sidekick-injected `/simplify` slash command. The skill is invocable from orchestrator, subagent, and foreign-agent contexts (Claude/Codex/Gemini) — eliminating the orchestrator-tier-only restriction that breaks the broken `code-simplifier` agent. Skill name MUST be exactly `simplify` (hard constraint — 27 downstream dispatch sites in 7bk.8 literal-reference the name).

A prior extraction left the slash command body at `~/.claude/skills/simplify/SKILL.md`; that file has been moved (during this brainstorm session) to `src/user/.agents/skills/simplify/SKILL.md`. Implementation REFINES that body — the extracted body is missing the R4 disciplines and they must be ADDED, not assumed-present.

## Background / Motivation

- **Slash commands are orchestrator-only.** Subagents cannot fire them, breaking dispatch from `ralf-it`, `completion-gate`, plugin formula TOMLs, and other skills.
- **`/simplify` lives outside this repo** (sidekick injection — see 7bk.7 NOTES). Not portable to non-sidekick installs or to Codex/Gemini.
- **The `code-simplifier` agent is broken.** It instructs itself to invoke a `simplify` skill that did not exist. Phantom dependency.
- **A skill at `src/user/.agents/skills/simplify/`** ships via `install.sh` to all three CLIs and works in any context.
- **Foundation work for the 7bk.8 sweep**, which renames 27 dispatch sites across 11 files to use this skill.

## Requirements

### R1 — Skill file exists with locked name
`src/user/.agents/skills/simplify/SKILL.md` exists with frontmatter:
- `name: simplify` (LOCKED — install.sh stages by directory name; runtime hosts read the frontmatter `name`. Both must be `simplify`.)
- `description: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.`

Schema follows existing skills (test-review, ralf-it as templates).

### R2 — Three-phase body, extracted reviewer checklist preserved verbatim
- **Phase 1 — Identify changes**: `git diff` (or `git diff HEAD`); fall back to recently-modified files when no git changes.
- **Phase 2 — Review on three axes**: Reuse, Quality, Efficiency. Reviewer checklist preserved VERBATIM from the extracted body (`src/user/.agents/skills/simplify/SKILL.md` lines 18–50).
- **Phase 3 — Aggregate + fix**: Apply behavior-preserving fixes; flag risky items with reason; do not argue with findings.

### R3 — Capability-shaped Phase-2 dispatch
Phase 2 instructs the executing agent in CAPABILITY terms, not tool names:

> "If your runtime supports concurrent subagent dispatch in a single turn (Claude `Agent`, Codex `Task`, Gemini equivalents), dispatch the three reviewers in parallel. Otherwise, execute the three reviews serially in this context."

Both paths produce equivalent findings (caveat: serial execution can carry context bias across axes — accepted, not addressed).

### R4 — Operating Discipline section (NEW — must be ADDED to extracted body)
The extracted body is MISSING all R4 disciplines. Implementation MUST add a new `## Operating Discipline` section (placed between the intro and Phase 1, or equivalent) containing:

- **Worktree scoping** — when the caller supplies a worktree path, operate there (not the main tree). When unspecified, operate in the current working directory.
- **Apply-vs-flag classification** with explicit category lists:
  - **Apply**: renames, dead-code removal, trivially-redundant-branch collapse, clearly-duplicated helper extraction.
  - **Flag**: anything that could change a return value, a side effect, an error path, an exported API, or runtime ordering.
- **No abstractions without ≥2 call sites** — new helpers, type aliases, wrappers require concrete evidence of duplication. "Might be reused later" is not evidence.
- **Boundary rules** — no scope creep into untouched files, no test rewrites, no new dependencies, no commits/pushes (the orchestrator owns delivery).
- **Memory-persistence guidance with no-memory fallback** — instruct the host agent to persist recurring patterns via the host's memory mechanism (project memory, equivalents). **Fallback when host has no memory mechanism**: surface the pattern in the final summary under a `Recurring pattern observed:` line so the user can record it manually.

### R5 — Output format
Terse summary, one of:
- **Simplifications applied** — list each with `path:line` and one-line "what".
- **Flagged for review** — list each with `path:line`, candidate change, reason held back.
- **None warranted** — say so explicitly; do not invent work.

If a recurring pattern was persisted, append: `> Memory updated: <one-line description>`. If the host has no memory mechanism, the pattern appears under `Recurring pattern observed:` in the summary instead.

### R6 — Source-of-truth note as inline comment header
Co-located `AGENTS.md` was rejected during spec review (hosts only read tree-root `AGENTS.md`; a per-skill file would ship to three install destinations and be read by zero of them).

Instead, the skill file itself carries a comment header recording:
- **Source**: `/simplify` slash command (sidekick injection — outside this repo).
- **Drift policy**: (C) accept periodic re-sync as known cost.
- **Last sync**: 2026-05-02.

The comment must NOT corrupt YAML frontmatter parsing. Recommended placement: HTML comment immediately AFTER the closing `---` of the frontmatter and BEFORE the H1, e.g.:

```markdown
---
name: simplify
description: ...
---
<!-- Source: /simplify slash command (sidekick injection); Drift policy: (C) periodic re-sync; Last sync: 2026-05-02 -->

# Simplify: Code Review and Cleanup
```

Implementer should verify by inspecting one existing skill that this comment placement does not break frontmatter parsing on any of the three host runtimes.

## Out of Scope

- **Renaming the 27 dispatch sites** across 11 files — `agents-config-7bk.8`.
- **Deleting `src/user/.agents/agents/code-simplifier.md`** — `agents-config-7bk.7` (blocked on 7bk.8).
- **Editing the `/simplify` slash command body in sidekick** — drift policy is (C), not (B).
- **Updating `README.md` agent enumeration** — handled by 7bk.7.
- **Tests / typecheck / lint scaffolding** — docs-only repo; AC honored vacuously.
- **Per-skill `AGENTS.md` convention for other skills** — if the inline comment header proves valuable, generalize via a separate bead later. Not this one.
- **Drift mitigation revisit (option B — rewrite slash command to invoke skill)** — acknowledged as the only fix that actually eliminates drift, but defer to a separate bead.

## Design Notes

### Drift mitigation: option (C) chosen, surfaced via inline comment header
User chose (C) periodic re-sync. The original spec proposed a co-located `AGENTS.md` in the skill directory; spec review (wisp-czl) rejected this — hosts read `AGENTS.md` only at tree roots, not inside skill directories, so a per-skill `AGENTS.md` ships to three locations and is read by zero. Replaced with an HTML comment header at the top of the skill body, where it co-locates with the source it describes and is visible to anyone editing the skill.

### Capability detection: capability-shaped, not tool-named
Original spec said "if Agent tool available." That binds to Claude's tool naming and silently forces Codex/Gemini into the serial branch. Reworded to runtime-capability terms ("concurrent subagent dispatch in a single turn") with explicit examples for each host. Same pattern as `ralf-it/SKILL.md`.

### Memory: host-agnostic with explicit no-memory fallback
Skills don't carry agent-style `memory: project` frontmatter (host-agnostic). The body instructs the host to use its own memory mechanism. **What if the host has no memory at all?** Original spec was silent — patterns silently vanished. Now: surface the pattern in the final summary so the user can record it manually. Lossy but honest.

### Why the inline comment, not docs/specs/
Spec review suggested either inline comment OR `docs/specs/` one-pager. Inline wins because: (a) zero distance to the source it describes; (b) future agents editing the skill see the sync date without context-switching; (c) no risk of the docs/specs file orphaning if the skill moves.

### Already-extracted body and the R4 trap
The extracted body at `src/user/.agents/skills/simplify/SKILL.md` is the slash-command Phase-2 checklist verbatim and NOTHING ELSE. R4 disciplines (worktree scoping, apply/flag classification, ≥2 call-sites rule, boundary rules, memory guidance) are ABSENT. An implementer reading R2 ("preserved verbatim") plus inspecting the body could declare Phase 2 done and skip R4. The acceptance criteria explicitly call out the new `Operating Discipline` section to prevent this trap.

### install.sh dry-run AC pinned to all three tools
Original AC said "no collisions" without specifying which tools to install for. An implementer running `--tools=claude` could miss a Codex/Gemini collision. AC #7 now pins the flags: `--tools=claude,codex,gemini --plugins=beads`.

### Findings vs equivalence in serial mode
Spec review noted that serial Phase-2 execution can bias later reviewers with earlier findings. Acknowledged but not addressed — the cost of caveating the spec exceeds the practical impact at three review axes.

## Open Questions

None blocking. Two deferred concerns documented above (drift policy revisit; per-skill AGENTS.md generalization).

## Coordination

- **Blocks**: `agents-config-7bk.8` (the sweep cannot run until this skill exists).
- **Adjacent**: `agents-config-7bk.7` (now blocked on 7bk.8 → blocked transitively on this bead).
- **Edits confined** to `src/user/.agents/skills/simplify/`. No other source files mutate.
close_reason: Implemented; PR #23 awaiting merge authorization. Skill at src/user/.agents/skills/simplify/SKILL.md, all 9 ACs verified, 3 Copilot inline comments addressed in dc031cf and threads resolved.

comments.author: Scott Hamilton

The implementer's first move should be to add it to their feature branch via 'git add' before the R4/R6 refinements. If the file is missing (e.g. fresh checkout on another machine), re-extract from the harness 'simplify' skill or rebuild from the spec.

See docs/specs/2026-04-26-pr-review-skill-redesign.md for the full design. Naming decision: keep wait-for-pr-comments; rename resolve-pr-comments to reply-and-resolve-pr-threads. title: Agents fail to claim beads at start-of-work and fail to close parent epics; discovered-work guidance misses epic-sibling placement
design: Investigation bead — findings and proposal phase. Implementation would touch 6 files: brainstorm-bead.formula.toml, implement-feature.formula.toml, fix-bug.formula.toml, merge-and-cleanup.formula.toml, start-bead/SKILL.md, create-bead/SKILL.md, plus rules/beads.md for the invariants section.
notes:
# Findings (audit of src/plugins/beads/** and src/user/.agents/skills/**)

## GAP 1 — Claim at start-of-work is missing in the brainstorm path

**Where the claim happens today:**
| File | Step | Claims bead? | Claims parent? |
|------|------|--------------|----------------|
| src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/create-bead/SKILL.md | all | NO (correct — create is pure capture) | n/a |
| src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/start-bead/SKILL.md | Route A | delegates to implement-bead | via implement-bead |
| src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/start-bead/SKILL.md | Route B (trivial inline) | **NO — MISSING** | **NO — MISSING** |
| src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/start-bead/SKILL.md | Route C (brainstorm) | **NO — MISSING** | **NO — MISSING** |
| src/plugins/beads/.beads/formulas/brainstorm-bead.formula.toml | all steps (assess → finalize) | **NO — MISSING** | **NO — MISSING** |
| src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/implement-bead/SKILL.md | Step 3 (after pour) | YES | YES (one-line mention) |
| src/plugins/beads/.beads/formulas/implement-feature.formula.toml | worktree step (step 2) | YES | YES |
| src/plugins/beads/.beads/formulas/fix-bug.formula.toml | worktree step (step 3, after diagnose) | YES | brief mention |

**Root cause of observed lu3.4 bug:** The brainstorm-bead formula has zero claim step. start-bead Route C (the path for beads needing brainstorming) also doesn't claim. The child is worked on for the entire brainstorming arc (assess → discuss → write-spec → review → finalize) with status=open. Parent epic is never marked in_progress either.

## GAP 2 — Parent-epic closure is phrased as observation, not as a command

**Current housekeeping language (implement-feature, fix-bug, merge-and-cleanup):**
```
2. If all siblings in a parent epic are now closed, close the parent:
     bd show {{bead-id}}    # check for parent
     bd list --parent=<parent-id>  # check siblings
```

**Problems:**
- No concrete close command shown; agent has to infer it.
- No recursion up the parent chain (an epic may be a child of another epic).
- No handling for the 'all siblings closed but one deferred' edge case.
- The rules/beads.md one-liner 'After close → if all siblings closed, close parent recursively' is correct but not echoed in the formula steps.

**start-bead Route B (trivial inline)** says 'Close the bead when done' with no parent guidance.

## GAP 3 — Discovered-work guidance doesn't prefer epic-sibling placement

**Current guidance defaults to orphan + discovered-from dep:**
- rules/beads.md: 'Discovered work → create bead NOW with discovered-from:<parent-id> dep'
- implement-feature housekeeping step 4: `bd create ... && bd dep add <new-id> {{bead-id}} --type discovered-from`
- fix-bug housekeeping step 4: same pattern
- implement-bead skill 'Discovered work' note: 'create new beads immediately' — no placement guidance

**Missing rule:** if the work-in-progress is inside an epic AND the discovered work is logically a sibling subtask of that epic, create with `--parent <epic-id>` so it lands in the epic, not as an orphan with only a dep link.

create-bead skill DOES document the `--parent <epic-id>` option but only in the 'child tasks under an epic' use case. Nothing tells discovering agents to prefer that placement.

---

# Proposed enhancements (concrete, reviewable)

## E1 — brainstorm-bead formula: add explicit claim step
New first step `claim` (before `assess`):
```toml
[[steps]]
id = "claim"
title = "Claim bead and parent epic"
description = """
Mark this bead and any parent-chain epics as in_progress before any work.

  bd update {{bead-id}} --status in_progress

  # Walk parent chain; mark each ancestor epic in_progress
  PARENT=$(bd show {{bead-id}} --json | jq -r '.parent // empty')
  while [ -n "$PARENT" ]; do
    bd update $PARENT --status in_progress
    PARENT=$(bd show $PARENT --json | jq -r '.parent // empty')
  done
"""
```
Mark `assess` as `needs = ["claim"]`.

## E2 — start-bead Route B (trivial inline): claim + close with parent walk
Add to Route B description: 'Before starting: claim bead + walk parent chain. After finishing: close bead + walk parent chain to close any epic whose remaining children are all closed.'

## E3 — Promote parent-epic closure from observation to command
In housekeeping steps of implement-feature, fix-bug, merge-and-cleanup, replace the fuzzy paragraph with:
```bash
# Recursive parent-epic close
PARENT=$(bd show {{bead-id}} --json | jq -r '.parent // empty')
while [ -n "$PARENT" ]; do
  OPEN=$(bd list --parent=$PARENT --status=open --json | jq 'length')
  [ "$OPEN" = "0" ] || break
  bd close $PARENT --reason "All children closed"
  PARENT=$(bd show $PARENT --json | jq -r '.parent // empty')
done
```

## E4 — Discovered-work: prefer epic-sibling placement
Update create-bead skill Step 2 with a new sub-section 'Placement of discovered work':
- If you're inside an in-progress bead that has a parent epic AND the new work is logically a sibling subtask of that epic → `bd create ... --parent <epic-id>`
- Otherwise → orphan + `bd dep add <new-id> <current-id> --type discovered-from`

Update implement-feature and fix-bug housekeeping step 4 to reflect this:
```bash
PARENT=$(bd show {{bead-id}} --json | jq -r '.parent // empty')
if [ -n "$PARENT" ] && <is-sibling-subtask>; then
  bd create "..." -t <type> -p <prio> --parent $PARENT
else
  bd create "..." -t <type> -p <prio>
  bd dep add <new-id> {{bead-id}} --type discovered-from
fi
```

## E5 — rules/beads.md: crystallize invariants
Replace the three-line 'Parent/child workflow' stub with a named 'Parent-chain invariants' section that states:
1. Child claim → walk parent chain, set each ancestor epic to in_progress.
2. Child close → walk parent chain, close each ancestor epic whose remaining children are all closed.
3. Discovered work under an in-progress epic → create with `--parent <epic-id>` (sibling placement) when it's logically a sibling subtask; otherwise orphan + discovered-from.

---

# Status
Findings complete. Proposed enhancements documented. Awaiting user review before implementation.

title: Divergence 4: brainstorm → implementation auto-transition (hand off to run-queue by default)
description:
# Context

Observed in the current lu3 session (and foreshadowed by lu3.1 / lu3.2): after brainstorming completes and the bead is marked `implementation-ready`, the natural flow of the agent — combined with superpowers momentum (TDD, writing-plans, subagent-driven-development) — pulls the user straight into implementation in the **same session**.

The `implement-feature` and `fix-bug` formulas are explicitly intended to be processed by the `run-queue` skill in a **dedicated session** (see beads.md Session Separation rule). Continuing in-session defeats that separation and makes run-queue redundant.

# Root cause (hypothesis)

1. `brainstorm-bead.formula.toml` finalize step says "It will be picked up by the next run-queue cycle, **or** you can start it manually with the implement-bead skill" — the word "or" reads as a choice rather than a hand-off signal.
2. `start-bead` Route C has no post-brainstorm pause: after the brainstorm formula completes, the agent is still "on" the bead with no explicit boundary telling it to stop.
3. `start-bead` Route A fires unconditionally when `implementation-ready` is present. This is the actual mechanism by which in-session continuation happens — the agent re-enters start-bead and hits A silently, bypassing the finalize-step message. (Surfaced by adversarial review of this bead's own spec.)
4. `implement-bead`'s "When to Use" lists "User explicitly says 'implement bead xyz' after brainstorming is complete" — this is too permissive; completing brainstorming in the same session is easily misread as implicit authorization.
5. Superpowers skills (writing-plans → subagent-driven-development etc.) create downstream pull even after lu3.2's partnership fences — they flow from brainstorming unless explicitly stopped at the bead boundary.
6. `run-queue` has no mirror guard against the opposite drift (a run-queue session brainstorming a spec gap inline) beyond existing Red Flags.

# Edits required

## 1. `src/plugins/beads/.beads/formulas/brainstorm-bead.formula.toml`

In the finalize step's Report-to-the-user block (currently lines 186–194), replace the existing report text with a hand-off-by-default message while PRESERVING the `Acceptance criteria: [list]` echo and the subsequent `bd mol burn` step (step 4). The new message:

    Report to the user:
      "Bead {{bead-id}} is now implementation-ready.
       Acceptance criteria: [list]

       This session stops here. Do NOT continue to implementation in this
       session unless you explicitly authorize it. The default path is:
       another agent (run-queue, in a dedicated session) picks up the
       implementation.

       If you want to implement now in the current session instead, say so
       explicitly ("implement it now", "do lu3.N next", etc.) and I will
       invoke `implement-bead`."

    Then STOP. Do not invoke implement-bead unless the user replies with
    explicit authorization within this session.

## 2. `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/start-bead/SKILL.md` — Route A

Add an authorization gate to Route A. Replace "Action: invoke the `implement-bead` skill directly." with:

    Action: check session context. If the `implementation-ready` label was
    applied in THIS session (e.g. the brainstorm formula just finalized this
    bead), STOP and require explicit user authorization before invoking
    `implement-bead`. Otherwise invoke `implement-bead` directly.

    The default in a just-brainstormed scenario is hand-off to run-queue.
    Ask the user only when intent is unclear; silent continuation is not
    permitted.

## 3. `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/start-bead/SKILL.md` — Route C

Append a Hand-off block at the bottom of Route C (after the execution loop and failure-mode section):

    ### Hand-off: stop at implementation-ready

    When the brainstorm formula completes and the bead has `implementation-ready`
    and `brainstormed` labels, STOP. The default path is: another agent
    (run-queue, in a dedicated session) picks up the implementation.

    Do NOT invoke `implement-bead` in this session unless the user:
    - Explicitly directs you to ("implement it now", "do X next"), OR
    - Explicitly agrees when you ask.

    Do NOT re-invoke `start-bead` on this bead in this session — that would
    route through Route A and bypass this hand-off.

    Asking is acceptable when the user's intent is unclear. Silent assumption
    that brainstorming means "also implement" is not.

Append a Red Flag row to the existing Red Flags table:

    | "Brainstorming is done, I'll implement next as a natural continuation" | No. Default is hand-off to run-queue. Stop unless explicitly authorized. |

## 4. `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/implement-bead/SKILL.md`

Tighten the "When to Use" list. Replace:

    - User explicitly says "implement bead xyz" after brainstorming is complete

with:

    - User explicitly and affirmatively authorizes implementation in this
      session (e.g. "implement it now", "yes, go"). Completing brainstorming
      in the same session is NOT implicit authorization.

Append a Red Flag row to the existing Red Flags table:

    | "Brainstorming finished cleanly, so the user must want implementation" | No. Hand off to run-queue by default. Ask or wait for explicit authorization. |

## 5. `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/run-queue/SKILL.md`

Append a mirror Red Flag row to the existing Red Flags table, guarding the opposite-direction drift (a run-queue session brainstorming inline):

    | "The bead isn't quite implementation-ready, I'll brainstorm the gap inline" | No. run-queue processes implementation-ready beads only. If the spec has gaps, flag the bead for re-brainstorming and move on. |

Match the wording/tone to the file's existing Red Flag phrasing.

## 6. `src/plugins/beads/.claude/rules/beads.md`

In the Session Separation section (currently lines 91–98), add a new bullet:

    **Post-brainstorm hand-off**: Any bead newly labeled `implementation-ready`
    in the current session is a hand-off candidate by default — regardless of
    how the label was set (brainstorm formula, manual `bd label add`, or
    imported). Implementation runs in a separate run-queue session.
    Continuing to implementation in the current session requires explicit
    user authorization per session.

# Design decisions

- **Default is hand-off, not implementation.** Aligns with the Session Separation rule already in beads.md: brainstorming session ≠ implementation session. Continuing in-session is an authorized exception, not the path of least resistance.
- **Ask-or-wait, not always-wait.** Asking the user at the boundary is fine ("Want me to implement now, or hand off to run-queue?"). What's not fine is silent continuation.
- **Reinforce at SIX layers** (formula finalize step, start-bead Route A gate, start-bead Route C hand-off block, implement-bead When-to-Use, run-queue mirror Red Flag, beads.md Session Separation). Defense-in-depth because the momentum toward implementation is strong.
- **Rule applies to the label, not the producer.** The beads.md bullet is phrased around "newly labeled `implementation-ready` in this session" so it catches hand-labeled and imported beads, not just brainstorm-bead output.
- **Route A is the primary breach site.** The finalize-step message alone is insufficient — once the label is applied, Route A silently re-routes any subsequent start-bead invocation straight into implement-bead. The Route A gate closes that path.

# Acceptance criteria

- `brainstorm-bead.formula.toml` finalize step's user-facing report explicitly says the session stops here and run-queue picks up by default; preserves the `Acceptance criteria: [list]` echo; preserves the `bd mol burn` step (step 4); and closes with an imperative "Then STOP" instruction to the agent.
- `start-bead` Route A has an authorization gate that blocks silent invocation of `implement-bead` when the `implementation-ready` label was applied in the current session.
- `start-bead` Route C has a Hand-off block at the end with the "default is STOP" rule, explicitly prohibits same-session re-entry through `start-bead`, and has a Red Flag row against natural-continuation bias.
- `implement-bead` When-to-Use tightens the brainstorming-continuation path and adds a matching Red Flag row.
- `run-queue` SKILL.md has a mirror Red Flag row guarding against brainstorming inline in a run-queue session.
- `beads.md` Session Separation has a new Post-brainstorm hand-off bullet phrased around the label (not the producer), so it catches manual and imported cases.
- No regressions to existing formula/skill/rule content; all edits are additive or replace exact text as spec'd.
- Build / typecheck / tests pass (vacuous for this docs-only repo — manual review of the six files is the real bar).

# Notes

- Discovered during session `eed3f46f` (the lu3 work itself) — the session went brainstorm → implementation immediately because no boundary existed. Exactly the pattern this bead fixes.
- Related to lu3.1 (materialize formula steps), lu3.2 (partnership contract fences methodology skills), and lu3.3 (delivery action categories). This one specifically targets the brainstorm→implement seam inside the bead lifecycle.
- Root-level template files (`src/user/.claude/rules/delegation.md`, root CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md templates) are intentionally out of scope. This is a plugin-scoped fix.
- Implementer note: any `\"` sequences in this description are markdown-storage artifacts. Emit plain double-quotes (`"`) in the actual file edits.
- Red Flag rows are appended to their existing tables; GFM does not require column alignment, so visual alignment is not a criterion.
- Dogfooding: this bead itself is subject to the rule it defines — when brainstorming of this bead completes, hand off to run-queue in a dedicated session; do not continue in-session without explicit authorization.
- Adversarial review cycle 1 surfaced (and this revision addresses): Route A gap, run-queue scope, verbatim-preservation of the formula's AC-echo/mol-burn lines, label-centric wording of the beads.md bullet, "Then STOP" in AC, root-template scope statement, escaped-quote disclaimer, and dogfooding note. Cycle 2 was not run; the remaining items were mechanical polish, not spec-destabilizing.
acceptance_criteria: brainstorm-bead.formula.toml finalize step's user-facing report: session stops here, run-queue picks up by default, preserves Acceptance criteria echo, preserves bd mol burn step, closes with imperative 'Then STOP'. start-bead Route A: authorization gate blocks silent implement-bead invocation when implementation-ready label was applied in the current session. start-bead Route C: Hand-off block at end with 'default is STOP' rule, explicit prohibition on same-session re-entry through start-bead, and appended Red Flag row against natural-continuation bias. implement-bead When-to-Use: tightened brainstorming-continuation path with matching Red Flag row. run-queue SKILL.md: mirror Red Flag row guarding against inline brainstorming in a run-queue session. beads.md Session Separation: new Post-brainstorm hand-off bullet phrased around the label (not the producer), catching manual/imported cases. No regressions to existing formula/skill/rule content; edits are additive or replace exact spec'd text. Build/typecheck/tests pass (vacuous for this docs-only repo — manual review of the six files is the real bar).
close_reason: Implemented and PR #12 merged (squash, commit 05d745e, merged 2026-04-19T18:50:11Z). Six reinforcement sites for brainstorm→implementation hand-off shipped. Session-label mechanism (implementation-readied-session-<sid>) provides mechanical Route A gating. Two Copilot review rounds addressed.

title: Split ralf-it into ralf-review + ralf-implement; push outer-workflow out of both
description:
# Context

`ralf-it` is currently a top-level skill in `src/user/.agents/skills/ralf-it/`. Session audit `c06c7b55` (Divergence 3 analysis) identified two problems with this form:

1. **Auto-invocation magnetism**: as a skill, `ralf-it` competes with bead-driven workflows the same way `writing-plans`, `executing-plans`, and `subagent-driven-development` do. It gets matched by the agent's "what skill fits this task?" scanner and pulled in even when a bead formula should own the flow.

2. **Over-specifies outer-workflow concerns**: its SKILL.md declares `using-git-worktrees` and `finishing-a-development-branch` as "REQUIRED SUB-SKILLS" — but those are outer-workflow concerns that conflict with bead formulas when `ralf-it` is invoked from inside a formula step. The formula already owns worktree isolation (via `implement-bead` setup) and delivery (via a later formula step).

The underlying RALF methodology — red-green-refactor iteration, fresh-eyes subagent review cycles, quality-gate passes — is valuable. The question is: **what FORM should it take to eliminate the friction without losing the methodology?**

# Open design questions

## Q1: What form should ralf-it take?

- **Skill (current)**: auto-invokable, magnetic, competes with formulas. Problematic.
- **Command (`/ralf-it`)**: user-invoked only, no auto-match. Eliminates magnetism. Still usable standalone.
- **Agent (or set of agents)**: dispatched by orchestrator. Slots cleanly inside formula steps as "dispatch the ralf-it agent."
- **Hybrid**: keep the methodology as a reusable reference (doc or molecule/formula), invoke via different entry points for different contexts.

## Q2: How do bead formulas embed the RALF methodology?

If we restructure ralf-it out of skill form, bead formulas must explicitly embed the RALF pattern. Places this matters:

- **`brainstorm-bead.formula.toml`** — already has a `ralf-spec-review` step (fresh-eyes review of written spec). Demonstrates the inner-only pattern. ✓
- **`implement-feature.formula.toml`** — needs a RALF loop step for the implementation (red-green-refactor + fresh-eyes refinement cycles + quality gate)
- **`fix-bug.formula.toml`** — same pattern as implement-feature, plus systematic-debugging up front

Two sub-options:
- **Inline the RALF pattern as multiple formula steps** (decompose it)
- **Create a RALF sub-formula/molecule** that other formulas reference (composable)

## Q3: What about non-bead, standalone use?

A user running `/ralf-it` on a quick bug fix without creating a bead still needs the full methodology. Whatever form we pick must support standalone use without forcing a bead workflow.

## Q4: Outer-workflow concerns

Regardless of form, ralf-it should be **inner-methodology only** — it iterates on CODE, not on worktrees or delivery. Who owns the outer loop?

- If called from a formula: the formula
- If called standalone: the user (or the command's documentation tells them what to set up first)

# Constraints

- Must not break standalone use (power users running `/ralf-it this bug` without a bead should still get the methodology)
- Must eliminate auto-match magnetism so it stops competing with bead formulas
- Must not require agents to remember "don't use ralf-it when on a bead" (Divergence 2 proved that rule-only guardrails leak)
- Existing `brainstorm-bead`'s `ralf-spec-review` step is the reference implementation of the inner-only pattern

# Trigger

Not implementation-ready. Requires brainstorming to decide form. Start after:
- `agents-config-lu3.1` is merged AND
- `agents-config-lu3.2` is merged AND
- Observation period long enough to see whether ralf-it friction persists in practice

# Brainstorm inputs (for future session)

- Current SKILL.md structure: 9-step workflow including worktree setup (Step 3) and delivery handoff (Step 9)
- Other prompts in the dir: `foreign-agent-prompt.md`, `foreign-eyes-prompt.md`, `fresh-eyes-prompt.md`, `implementer-prompt.md` — these are the subagent prompts ralf uses
- Partnership contract precedent: `writing-plans`/`executing-plans`/`subagent-driven-development` chosen as "off-limits" — ralf-it currently evades that list because we didn't think to add it
- Related: agents-config-717 (risk-closer evaluation) — ralf-it reform could be one of the risk-closers

# Acceptance criteria (TBD during brainstorm)

- `bd decision` recorded on ralf-it's new form (skill / command / agent / hybrid) with rationale
- If restructuring: formulas updated to embed RALF methodology inline, or a reusable RALF molecule/formula created
- If keeping as skill: skill is cleaned to be workflow-agnostic (no REQUIRED outer-workflow skills), red flag added to prevent peer invocation on beads
- Standalone use path documented and preserved
- Build passes. Typecheck passes. Tests pass.

# Notes

- NOT blocked by lu3.1/lu3.2 (can start independently), but best run after those land so we can observe ralf-it behavior in the fixed pipeline
- This is a DESIGN question — route through `start-bead` → `brainstorm-bead` formula when ready
- Session `c06c7b55` flagged this as a cleaner fix than patching ralf-it's workflow declarations in place
