title: [merge-or-handoff] {{title-slug}}
description:
Either auto-merge the PR or hand off to the human.
Model: claude-haiku-4-5  Effort: medium

## Gate 1 — PR-state clean-check
Conditions (ALL must be true for auto-merge path):
  - Zero open Copilot review threads
  - Zero open human-author review threads
  - All CI checks green
  - 5-minute quiet window with no new comments or review activity
On wait-for-pr-comments timeout: treat clean-check as NOT clean.

## Auto-merge path (auto-mergeable label present AND clean-check clean)
1. Re-run clean-check immediately before merge call.
2. Pour merge-and-cleanup formula (phase: liquid — NOT vapor).
3. Drive merge-and-cleanup:
   - Merge the PR
   - Delete branch (local + remote)
   - Remove worktree (decode from worktree-path-* label)
   - Run parent-chain I2 close-walk
   Note: merge-and-cleanup gate-step (Gate 2) blocks until ALL [h] follow-up
   children are closed with verified-by-human label, then closes the
   merge-{source-id} child after merge + cleanup so the source bead can close.

## Hand-off path (default — auto-mergeable absent OR clean-check dirty)
1. Transition bead to open: bd update {{bead-id}} --status open
2. Stamp labels: bd label add {{bead-id}} merge-ready
                 bd label add {{bead-id}} human
   (merge-ready = why it's on human list; human = surfaces via bd human list)
3. Note: bead is now excluded from bd ready (human label gates it).
   Human runs /merge-and-cleanup when ready.

## Idempotent re-entry
Query gh pr view --json mergedAt first. If non-null: skip merge, proceed to cleanup.
notes: Gate 1: 1 open thread (PRRT_kwDORA3vz86CKBeB = C5 SKIP, intentional non-resolution per SKIP contract). No auto-mergeable label. Hand-off path taken. PR #73 is merge-ready.
close_reason: merge-or-handoff: hand-off path. merge-ready + human labels stamped. Awaiting explicit merge authorization.

title: [create-pr] {{title-slug}}
description:
Create the pull request and persist its URL to the molecule.
Model: claude-haiku-4-5  Effort: medium

## Preloaded skills
superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch

## Actions

1. Invoke superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch.

2. PR body MUST include:
   - Bead reference: {{bead-id}} and bead title
   - AC (full list with [m]/[h] tags)
   - implementation iteration summary (from green-loop step-bead notes)
   - verify-ac validation report
   - Bug beads filed during verify-ac (if any)
   - Human-required AC follow-up bead references (if any [h] lines)
   - Foreign-eyes status section (list which iterations had degraded foreign-eyes)

3. Stamp pr-url-* label on molecule:
   Encoding: same bijection as worktree-path (though URLs rarely need it).
   bd label add <mol-id> pr-url-<encoded-url>

## PR creation failure
Flag-human with worktree intact. Do NOT tear down the worktree.

## Idempotent re-entry
Query gh pr view first. If a PR exists for the branch and is OPEN: skip creation,
persist URL idempotently.
If PR exists and is CLOSED: flag-human. Do NOT auto-reopen or create a new PR.

title: [verify-ac] {{title-slug}}
description:
Validate every [m]-tagged AC bullet against worktree state.
Model: claude-haiku-4-5  Effort: medium

Skips when review-level:none.

## Inputs
- Bead AC from: bd show {{bead-id}} (parse [m] and [h] tags)
- Worktree path from: worktree-path-* label on molecule
- Functional tests from: project-config.toml [functional-tests]

## Actions

For each [m]-tagged AC line:
1. Dispatch bead-verifier (haiku, low effort) to run shell-runnable witnesses.
   Collect: exit code + error excerpt.
2. Assemble validation report.

[h]-tagged AC lines: do NOT validate here. Confirm that human follow-up
child beads were filed at brainstorm-time finalize (bd list --parent {{bead-id}}
filtered by label human). If any [h] bullet has no matching follow-up, flag-human.

Run functional/e2e tests from [functional-tests] in project-config.toml.
If functional tests fail:
  - Create one bug bead per failure, label human.
  - Note in step-bead. Continue to create-pr (do NOT loop back).

Persist validation report to BOTH:
  - Step-bead notes: bd update <step-bead-id> --append-notes "<report>"
  - Source bead notes: bd update {{bead-id}} --append-notes "<report>"
  (Report also included in PR description at create-pr.)

## Definition of Done
Every [m] AC bullet has explicit witness evidence.
Human-review bullets have follow-up beads filed (confirm presence).

## Idempotent re-entry
Re-run re-evaluates and continues from prior pass.

title: [red-tests] {{title-slug}}
description:
Write failing tests (TDD red phase) before any implementation code.
Model: claude-sonnet-4-6  Effort: medium

## Preloaded skills
superpowers:test-driven-development, writing-unit-tests, testing-anti-patterns

## Inputs
- Bead AC from: bd show {{bead-id}} (read [m]-tagged lines)
- Worktree path: decode worktree-path-* label from molecule

## Decode worktree-path label before any work
  1. Decode: __ → /  then  _u → _
  2. Verify decoded path exists and is a git worktree:
       git -C <path> rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree
  3. Operate from that cwd.

## Actions

### Hard-escalate + auto-reroute (evaluate FIRST, before any reviewer dispatch)

This step has a canonical escalate predicate. Evaluate triggers in this fixed
order at the top of the step:

1. Trigger A — no test runner. If `[gates].test == ""` in project-config.toml,
   the project has no way to run tests:
     red-tests escalate: no test runner ([gates].test=='')

2. Trigger B — no [m] AC lines. If the bead's `acceptance_criteria`
   field (the canonical AC store per spec §4.1, NOT `description`) has
   zero `[m]`-classified lines, there is nothing for tests to assert.
   Apply the canonical line parser (`^\\[(m|h)\\]\\s(.*)$`; untagged lines
   default to `[m]` per §4.1's backwards-compat rule). Counting
   `description` would both false-positive on `[m]` tokens inside notes
   AND false-negative on canonical (non-bullet) AC formats. Pinned
   line-anchored grep -cvE counts every non-blank line that is NOT
   explicitly `[h]`-tagged (i.e. [m] lines plus untagged-defaults-to-[m]):
     m_count=$(bd show {{bead-id}} --json \\
       | jq -r '.[0].acceptance_criteria // ""' \\
       | grep -cvE '^([[:space:]]*$|\\[h\\][[:space:]])')
   If m_count == 0:
     red-tests escalate: no [m] AC lines

3. Both triggers fire (no test runner AND no [m] AC lines):
     red-tests escalate: both (no test runner AND no [m] AC lines)

4. Otherwise, proceed with the normal red-tests behavior below.

Conversely: a bead with [m] ACs and a non-empty test runner MUST run
red-tests normally — the escalate triggers are the only way to skip.
There is no per-bead `red-tests:enable` opt-IN or `red-tests:skip`
opt-OUT label. AC tagging + project [gates].test are the only signals.

### Reroute protocol (when any escalate trigger fires)

The bead is misrouted: an `implement-feature` molecule is running on work
that legitimately has no code tests. Auto-reroute to the `docs-only` formula
by cloning the bead — no `human` label, no manual intervention.

1. Determine the trigger note text from the predicate above:
     <trigger-note> = "Trigger A: no test runner" |
                       "Trigger B: no [m] AC lines" |
                       "Trigger A: no test runner AND Trigger B: no [m] AC lines"

2. Read the original bead's parent (if any) to preserve epic membership,
   and its priority (so the merge-gate child created in step 4 inherits it):
       ORIG_PARENT=$(bd show {{bead-id}} --json | jq -r '.[0].parent // empty')
       ORIG_PRIORITY=$(bd show {{bead-id}} --json | jq -r '.[0].priority // "P3"')

3. Create the new bead as a partial copy of the original:
   - Inherit: `title`, `description`, `acceptance_criteria` from {{bead-id}}.
     Compose the `--description` value as a two-LF-separated string:
       "⚠️ Rerouted from `{{bead-id}}` — formula corrected to `docs-only`."
       (blank line)
       <original description>
     Use `printf '%s\\n\\n%s' "<warning>" "<original>"` or the `$'...\\n\\n...'`
     form to ensure the LFs are real newlines, not literal `\\n` characters.
   - Do NOT inherit `notes` (the new bead starts clean; the original's
     notes contain stale design history).
   - Preserve epic linkage: if `ORIG_PARENT` is non-empty, pass
     `--parent "$ORIG_PARENT"` so the rerouted-clone remains under the
     same epic. The `discovered-from` dep edge below records the audit
     trail; the parent edge keeps epic-level reporting intact.
   - Stamp labels on the new bead: `brainstormed`, `formula-docs-only`,
     `implementation-ready`. `brainstormed` is honest here — the original
     bead was already brainstormed and the reroute clones its
     `acceptance_criteria` and `description` verbatim, so the spec carries
     over; only the formula tag changes. Stamping `brainstormed` also
     satisfies `docs-only`'s preflight (which strictly requires
     `brainstormed`) so the rerouted clone runs autonomously instead of
     flag-humaning on entry.
   - Do NOT stamp any `implementation-readied-session-*` label — the new
     bead is intended for a separate run-queue session, not for the
     session that just burned this molecule.
   - Add a discovered-from edge:
       bd dep add <new-bead-id> {{bead-id}} --type discovered-from

4. Create the merge-gate child for the new bead (mirrors brainstorm-bead.finalize §4.3 — without this, merge-and-cleanup gate 2 has nothing to enforce against, and the rerouted bead cannot complete its eventual PR cycle):
       bd create \
         --type task \
         --parent <new-bead-id> \
         --title "[Merge gate] <new-bead-title>" \
         --description "Closes when the merge-and-cleanup formula's merge step completes." \
         --priority "$ORIG_PRIORITY"
       # Capture the returned id and stamp the merge-gate label:
       bd label add <new-merge-gate-id> merge-gate

   `<new-bead-title>` is the title inherited from the original (already set
   on the new bead in step 3). Do NOT label `human` (system-managed gate
   child, not a human-attention item). Do NOT label `implementation-ready`
   (the shell driver ignores it).

5. Re-parent any open `[h]` follow-up children of the original bead to
   the new bead. Per spec §4.3, brainstorm-bead's finalize step files one
   `[h]` follow-up child for every `[h]` AC line. The new bead inherits
   `acceptance_criteria` (which still contains those `[h]` lines) but
   does NOT re-run brainstorm-bead's finalize. Re-parenting the children
   preserves the verify-ac contract on the new bead (verify-ac's "[h]
   bullet has matching follow-up" check still passes):
       for child in $(bd list --parent {{bead-id}} --label human --json \
         | jq -r '.[] | select(.status != "closed") | .id'); do
         bd update "$child" --parent <new-bead-id>
       done
   The original bead's merge-gate child is handled separately in step 8 —
   it is closed, not re-parented. The new bead's merge-gate child was
   already filed in step 4 above.

6. Stamp the original bead with the redirect marker:
       bd label add {{bead-id}} REROUTED-TO:<new-bead-id>

7. Append a note to the original bead:
       bd update {{bead-id}} --append-notes "Rerouted to <new-bead-id>. <trigger-note>. New bead created with formula-docs-only and implementation-ready. Run-queue will pick it up in a separate session."

8. Close any merge-gate child of the original bead (so the original can
   close cleanly). Iterate over all matches in case duplicates exist:
       bd list --parent {{bead-id}} --label merge-gate --json \
         | jq -r '.[] | select(.status != "closed") | .id' \
         | while read -r merge_child; do
             bd close "$merge_child" --reason "Parent bead rerouted."
           done

9. Close the original bead:
       bd close {{bead-id}} --reason "Rerouted to <new-bead-id> (<trigger-note>)."

10. Append a note to the current step-bead (forensic; persists in molecule
   audit even after burn). The literal prefix `red-tests escalate: ` is
   grep-able for verify-ac groundedness and for green-loop's
   defense-in-depth check:
       bd update <step-bead-id> --append-notes "red-tests escalate: <trigger-note>. Cloned to <new-bead-id> with formula-docs-only."

11. Burn the current molecule:
       bd mol burn <mol-id> --force

After burn, this stage and the entire molecule cease execution. Run-queue
in a separate session will pick up <new-bead-id> organically and pour the
`docs-only` formula on it. No human action required.

### Normal flow (when no escalate trigger fires)

1. Dispatch tdd-red-team (opus, high effort) in multi-AC mode to write tests:
   - Dispatch with: worktree path, AC bullets (all [m]-tagged lines),
     step-bead ID, project test-runner command, absolute report path,
     and the explicit input mode "multi-AC mode" (one or more failing
     tests per [m]-tagged AC bullet — see tdd-red-team.md for the
     multi-AC vs single-regression mode contract).
   - Tests must cover: happy path, edge cases, error paths.
   - Do NOT mock what has not been verified.
   - Do NOT write test-only methods in production code.
   - The worker emits a tdd-red-report-v1 YAML to the report path; the
     orchestrator parses it after exit.

2. Run the Claude reviewer (test-review skill) on the written tests.
   When review-level:none is set: skip test reviewers (tests still written).

3. Run Codex adversarial review of the tests (gpt-5.5 default).
   Model from: project-config.toml [foreign-cli].codex_red_tests_model
   When review-level:none: skip.
   Subject to foreign-eyes degradation tracking (FOREIGN-EYES-ITER-<n>).

4. Iteration cap: 2 (override: iteration-cap-red-tests-<n> label).
   At cap with reviewer disagreement: write disagreement note to step-bead notes.
   Escalate human ONLY if BOTH reviewers agree on a critical/major design flaw.

5. Commit failing tests to the feature branch.

## Definition of Done
Tests committed to feature branch; both reviewers approve "shippable" state
(or iteration cap reached with disagreement note recorded).
OR step rerouted per the canonical escalate predicate above: original bead
closed with `REROUTED-TO:<new-id>`, new bead created with
`formula-docs-only` + `implementation-ready`, current molecule burned, and
`red-tests escalate: ...` note appended to step-bead notes.

## Idempotent re-entry
Check filesystem (committed tests) and step-bead notes. Continue from prior state.
notes: tdd-red-team complete. T10 committed (591a6dc), fails on unfixed code as designed. Discovered work: agents-config-9a38 (pre-existing dep-health-check test failures block wrapped runner — green-loop must verify via direct `bash src/user/.agents/skills/whats-next/collect_test.sh` invocation as workaround until 9a38 fixed).
close_reason: tdd-red-team complete: T10 mode-isolation assertion committed and red. Audit label stamped. Discovered work filed as agents-config-9a38.

title: [merge-gate] installsh-support-plugin-exten
description: Merge gate child for agents-config-phzj.4. Closed only by merge-and-cleanup after merge + branch/worktree cleanup.

title: [Impl] install.sh: support plugin-to-base-asset extension via YAML patches
description:
# Spec: install.sh plugin-extension mechanism for base markdown assets (phzj.3) — rev 4 (final)

## Summary

A new install.sh staging step, `apply_extensions`, lets plugins surgically patch base markdown assets (skills, agents, or any other base `.md` file) via structured YAML manifests. Each yaml declares a target file, a target section (by header text, or the special keyword `frontmatter`), a precision verb, and the content to apply. The mechanism is format-agnostic, runs per enabled tool, composes multiple plugins/patches in deterministic order, and fails terminally on any target-resolution ambiguity or post-patch validation failure.

## Background / Motivation

Today install.sh has no mechanism for a plugin to extend a base SKILL.md (or any base markdown asset). `rules.md` collisions append with a `---` separator; SKILL.md / agent / commands collisions are fatal. The existing `carrier-merge` allows plugins to add disjoint sibling files into a shared skill directory but cannot edit the base SKILL.md itself.

Concrete pressure: `agents-config-acmh.5` specifies a `merge-and-cleanup` shared skill with an inline beads-specific cheat block delimited by `<!-- BEGIN beads-cheat — extract via agents-config-phzj.3 -->` / `<!-- END beads-cheat -->`. The block is inline only because no extension mechanism exists; once one lands, the block must be extractable to a plugin-owned overlay.

User-rejected alternative: mirroring the `rules.md` append-with-`---`-separator pattern. It cannot reproduce the inline cheat block's mid-document placement, conflates "this is an extension" with "this is a same-name file collision," and offers no per-patch declarative contract.

## Requirements

### R1 — Extension file location and scope

Extensions live in **scope-bearing** directories within the plugin tree:

- `src/plugins/<plugin>/.agents/extensions/*.yaml` — shared scope. Applies to every enabled tool's staging tree.
- `src/plugins/<plugin>/.claude/extensions/*.yaml` — Claude scope. Applies only to Claude staging.
- `src/plugins/<plugin>/.codex/extensions/*.yaml` — Codex scope. Applies only to Codex staging.
- `src/plugins/<plugin>/.gemini/extensions/*.yaml`, `.opencode/extensions/*.yaml` — analogous for those tools.

The directory declares the scope; the yaml's `target-file` field declares the asset within that scope.

`apply_extensions()` runs **once per enabled tool**, against that tool's per-tool staging tree (e.g., `staging/claude/`, `staging/codex/`). Shared-scope extensions are applied to every enabled tool's staging tree. Tool-scope extensions only to that tool's tree. Failure of any extension against any tool is terminal for the entire install — no partial sync to `~/.<tool>/`.

### R2 — Extension YAML schema

Required fields (no optional fields, no defaults). Top-level YAML MUST be a mapping (not a list or scalar):

```yaml
target-file: <path>            # relative to the per-tool staging root (e.g., "skills/merge-and-cleanup/SKILL.md")
target-section: <string>       # literal ATX-header text (whitespace-trimmed, LEADING hashes stripped, case-sensitive) OR the keyword "frontmatter"
precision: <verb>              # one of: replace | insert_before | insert_after | prepend | append
content: |                     # multi-line markdown (or YAML, when target-section is "frontmatter")
  ...
```

**Hash-strip is leading-only.** A header line `## Title ##` produces literal text `Title ##` (trailing decorations preserved verbatim). `target-section: "Title ##"` matches it; `target-section: "Title"` does NOT. This keeps the matching rule simple and unambiguous.

**Trailing whitespace is stripped from the header text BEFORE comparison.** A header line `## Title   ` (with trailing spaces) matches `target-section: "Title"`. Only leading whitespace is rejected (R3's regex requires `^(#{1,6}) (.+)$` with no leading whitespace before the hashes).

**One patch per yaml file.** A plugin needing N patches against the same base file ships N yaml files in its `extensions/` dir. Alphabetical filename ordering resolves same-target collisions.

### R3 — Target matching

1. Resolve `target-file` within the matching scope's per-tool staging root. File missing → terminal error.
2. Resolve `target-section`:
   - Value `frontmatter` → the target is the leading frontmatter block: the file MUST begin at byte 0 with a `---` line, followed by zero or more YAML body lines, followed by a closing `---` line. Any preceding content (BOM, blank line, shebang-like comment, anything) means no frontmatter exists → terminal error.
   - Else → search the file for matching ATX headers. A line is a recognized header iff ALL the following are true:
     - It matches the regex `^(#{1,6}) (.+)$` (between 1 and 6 hash marks, a single space, then any non-empty text). No leading whitespace.
     - It is NOT inside a fenced code block. A fenced code block opens on a line whose first non-whitespace content is exactly three or more backticks ` ``` ` or three or more tildes `~~~`, and closes on a line whose first non-whitespace content is the same fence character (backtick or tilde) at length ≥ the opener's length. **Indented (4-space) code blocks are NOT recognized as code** — base authors MUST avoid lines starting with `#` followed by a space inside indented examples. (A future hardening could replace this rule with a CommonMark parser; out of scope here.)
     - Its text — leading hashes and the single following space stripped, then trailing whitespace stripped — equals `target-section` exactly (case-sensitive).
3. Validate cardinality:
   - 0 matches → terminal error: `extension <yaml-path>: target-section "<value>" not found in <target-file>`.
   - ≥2 matches → terminal error: `extension <yaml-path>: target-section "<value>" appears <n> times in <target-file>; ambiguous`.
   - Exactly 1 → proceed. Record the matched header's hash-count as the section's depth **D**.

### R4 — Precision semantics, body-section target

A body section is bounded by its matched header (depth D) and the next ATX header at depth ≤ D — per R3's recognition rules (regex, no leading whitespace, not inside a fenced code block) — or EOF. Code-fenced regions are skipped — a `## Foo` inside a fenced code block does NOT terminate a section. A malformed pseudo-header like `##NoSpace` is not a recognized header per R3 and does not terminate a section either. If the matched section is the LAST section in the file (no next-section boundary exists), the section body extends to EOF; `replace` runs to EOF.

| Precision | Effect |
|---|---|
| `insert_before` | Insert content + `\n` immediately above the matched header line. |
| `insert_after` | Insert `\n` + content + `\n` immediately below the matched header line, before existing body. |
| `prepend` | Same physical position as `insert_after`. |
| `append` | Insert `\n` + content immediately after the LAST NON-BLANK line of the section body. All trailing blank lines that existed in the original section (between the last non-blank line and the next-section boundary) are preserved AFTER the inserted content. |
| `replace` | Replace the section body. The matched header line and the next-section boundary header are preserved. The replacement content terminates with `\n` if it does not already. |

**Worked examples for `append`:**

- No trailing blanks. Pre: `## Foo\nbody\n## Next\n`. Append `X`. Post: `## Foo\nbody\nX\n## Next\n`.
- Trailing blanks. Pre: `## Foo\nbody\n\n\n## Next\n`. Append `X`. Post: `## Foo\nbody\nX\n\n\n## Next\n`.
- Multi-line content. Pre: `## Foo\nbody\n## Next\n`. Append `X\nY`. Post: `## Foo\nbody\nX\nY\n## Next\n`.

`insert_after` and `prepend` are intentionally redundant (semantic taste, no install-time difference).

### R5 — Precision semantics, frontmatter target

Frontmatter is treated as a section whose "header" is the opening `---` delimiter and whose "body" is the YAML between delimiters. All five precisions apply symmetrically:

| Precision | Effect |
|---|---|
| `insert_before` | Insert content above the opening `---` (lands before the frontmatter entirely; rare/odd, allowed). **Side effect:** content now precedes the `---` line, so the file no longer has a leading frontmatter block by R3's "byte 0" rule. Any LATER `target-section: frontmatter` patch against the same file will fail per R3 (no leading frontmatter). Order yaml filenames accordingly. |
| `insert_after` | Insert content immediately below the opening `---`, before existing YAML keys. |
| `prepend` | Same physical position as `insert_after`. |
| `append` | Insert content at the end of the YAML body, immediately before the closing `---`. |
| `replace` | Replace YAML body between the delimiters; both `---` lines preserved. |

When `target-section: frontmatter`, plugin `content` is YAML (not markdown). For `replace`, the plugin supplies a complete YAML body (without the `---` delimiters).

**Missing-frontmatter handling.** All five precisions on `target-section: frontmatter` require a frontmatter block to exist (terminal error if absent). Rationale: the target IS the frontmatter block; without one, there is no anchor. Plugins wanting to add content above where frontmatter would land in a file lacking frontmatter MUST target the body of the file (e.g., the first body section) using a body-section precision.

**Post-patch YAML validation.** After any frontmatter mutation (`replace`, `insert_after`, `prepend`, `append`), the resulting frontmatter body MUST parse as valid YAML via `python3 -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(...)"`. Parse failure is terminal. (`insert_before` lands content above the frontmatter and does not mutate the YAML body — no re-parse required.)

### R6 — Multi-plugin / multi-extension ordering

Extensions applied in deterministic order, per enabled tool:

1. **Tool**: each enabled tool's staging tree processed independently (Claude, Codex, Gemini, OpenCode). A tool may receive shared-scope and its own tool-scope extensions; it never receives another tool's tool-scope extensions.
2. **Plugin**: plugins iterated in alphabetical order by plugin directory name.
3. **Scope within plugin**: shared-scope extensions (`.agents/extensions/`) apply BEFORE that plugin's tool-scope extensions (`.<tool>/extensions/`). Rationale: shared scope establishes the cross-tool foundation; tool scope layers tool-specific adjustments on top.
4. **Filename within scope**: yaml files iterated in alphabetical order by filename.

Each patch applies in sequence to the (potentially already-mutated) staged file. Later patches see earlier patches' effects, including any new headers earlier patches' content introduced — target-section resolution always runs against the CURRENT state of the file.

### R7 — Failure modes (all terminal)

install.sh exits non-zero with an error citing the relevant fields per the table below. On any terminal failure, the installer prints `no changes were synced to ~/.<tool>/` and aborts before any sync step.

| Failure class | Citation fields required |
|---|---|
| Malformed YAML in extension | extension yaml path; reason ("malformed YAML: <parser error>") |
| Top-level YAML is not a mapping | extension yaml path; reason ("top-level YAML is a <list/scalar>; mapping required") |
| Missing required field (`target-file`, `target-section`, `precision`, `content`) | extension yaml path; reason ("missing required field: <name>") |
| Unknown `precision` value | extension yaml path; reason ("unknown precision: <value>; expected one of: replace, insert_before, insert_after, prepend, append") |
| `target-file` missing in staging tree | extension yaml path; target-file path; reason ("target-file not found in staging tree") |
| `target-section` zero matches | extension yaml path; target-file; reason ("target-section \"<value>\" not found") |
| `target-section` ≥2 matches | extension yaml path; target-file; reason ("target-section \"<value>\" appears <n> times; ambiguous") |
| `target-section: frontmatter` but file has no leading frontmatter block | extension yaml path; target-file; reason ("target-section is frontmatter, but target-file has no leading frontmatter block") |
| Post-patch frontmatter body fails YAML parse | extension yaml path; target-file; reason ("post-patch frontmatter is not valid YAML: <parser error>") |
| Preflight: missing `python3` or PyYAML | none (preflight runs before any per-extension iteration); reason ("apply_extensions requires python3 with PyYAML; install with: pip install pyyaml") to stderr |

No partial application: any extension failure aborts the install before sync to `~/.<tool>/`.

### R8 — `--dry-run` validation

`install.sh --dry-run` runs `apply_extensions()` against the per-tool staging trees exactly as a real install does. The ONLY difference is the final `rsync` of staging → `~/.<tool>/` is skipped. There is no separate tmpdir codepath. The staging trees themselves are temporary directories that the script discards on exit; `--dry-run` simply exits before the sync.

A passing `--dry-run` proves the full extension set is composable, applicable, and produces valid YAML post-patch (where applicable). A failing extension in `--dry-run` reports the same error a real install would.

### R9 — Implementation placement

- New function `apply_extensions()` invoked from `stage_and_install_tool()` AFTER all base + carrier-merged + plugin content is staged, BEFORE the staging tree syncs to `~/.<tool>/`. Called once per enabled tool.
- Discovery: for the current tool, scan each enabled plugin for `.agents/extensions/` (shared scope) and `.<tool>/extensions/` (tool scope); collect `*.yaml`, sort per R6, apply each.
- All YAML parsing AND markdown section parsing handled by a Python helper script at `scripts/apply-extension.py` (flat layout, alongside `scripts/install.sh`). Python's `yaml` module (PyYAML) is the only added dependency.
- **Preflight:** `apply_extensions()` checks for `python3` and `python3 -c "import yaml"` BEFORE running; missing dependency is terminal with a clear error.
- **Helper CLI contract**: `scripts/apply-extension.py <extension-yaml-path> <target-file-path> <staging-root>`. On success: exit 0, no stdout/stderr required. On terminal failure: exit 1, single line to stderr matching one of the R7 citation patterns. install.sh shells out per patch and surfaces stderr verbatim.

### R10 — `--prune` compatibility

Extensions don't create orphan files; they mutate staged content already covered by the existing prune logic. No changes required to `--prune`.

## Out of Scope

- **Migration tooling** to auto-extract existing inline `<!-- BEGIN X -->` blocks into yaml extensions. Manual one-time work per asset; the mechanism is the deliverable.
- **Per-key surgical modification of frontmatter** (e.g., "add `Foo` to the `allowed-tools` array"). Frontmatter is mutable as a YAML body via the five precisions, but key-level merge semantics are not in scope. Plugins needing key-level edits use `replace` with a full YAML body, or `append` to add a new top-level key.
- **CommonMark parser** for full markdown spec compliance (indented code blocks, setext headers, alt fence rules). R3's ATX-only + fenced-code rules are a deliberate simplified subset; a future bead could replace the parser with `markdown-it-py` or `mistune` if base content evolves to require it.
- **Visual diff/preview** of what an extension WILL produce. Separate future bead.
- **Conflict detection beyond "duplicate target"**. Two plugins both `replace`-ing the same section won't error — last-write-wins per the R6 ordering rule. The installer cannot distinguish intentional layering from accidental collision; it enforces deterministic order and leaves intent-validation to plugin authors. Add a `--strict` flag if this hurts later.
- **Implementation of the merge-and-cleanup skill itself.** That work belongs to the Y bead produced by acmh.5. The extraction in AC #6 below assumes the inline cheat block exists at extraction time.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. **Schema validation** — install.sh rejects an extension yaml that is malformed YAML, has a non-mapping top-level, is missing any required field, or has an unknown `precision` value. Each error cites the fields required by R7's failure table.
2. **Target resolution** — install.sh rejects an extension whose `target-file` doesn't exist in the staging tree, OR whose `target-section` matches zero or ≥2 headers, OR whose `target-section: frontmatter` target lacks a leading frontmatter block. Each error cites per R7.
3. **Body-section precisions** — each of the five precisions produces the expected diff against fixture base files. Fixtures cover: trailing blank lines in section body, nested sub-sections at depth D+1 preserved during `append`, fenced code blocks containing `#` characters (not treated as headers), and `append` worked examples from R4 verified byte-exact.
4. **Frontmatter precisions** — all five precisions work on a `target-section: frontmatter` target. Fixture validates each, including `replace` preserves both `---` delimiters and resulting YAML re-parses cleanly. Cases without frontmatter are rejected per R5/R7.
5. **Ordering** — when extensions across (tool × plugin-alphabetical × scope-shared-before-tool × filename-alphabetical) target the same file, patches apply in that order and the final file content matches a known fixture. Fixture includes at least one case where ordering changes the result.
6. **Composition with introduced headers** — an extension whose `content` introduces a new ATX header that another later extension targets resolves correctly (later target resolution sees the new header). Fixture exercises both the "now-resolvable" case (was 0 matches without prior patch, now 1) and the "now-ambiguous" case (was 1 match without prior patch, now ≥2 → terminal). The symmetric "now-zero" case (a `replace` removes a header a later patch was targeting → 1→0 → terminal "not found") is covered by AC #2's zero-match coverage; no additional fixture required.
7. **Tool-scope isolation** — fixture demonstrates that a `.claude/extensions/*.yaml` patch is applied to Claude's staging only and does NOT appear in Codex's deployed output, while a `.agents/extensions/*.yaml` patch appears in both.
8. **Extraction performed** — `merge-and-cleanup` skill's inline beads cheat block extracted to a plugin overlay file at `src/plugins/beads/.agents/extensions/`. The base `src/user/.agents/skills/merge-and-cleanup/SKILL.md` no longer contains the inline cheat block OR the `<!-- BEGIN beads-cheat -->` / `<!-- END beads-cheat -->` marker comments.
9. **Extraction round-trip equivalence** — deployed `~/.claude/skills/merge-and-cleanup/SKILL.md` after extension application contains, at the original cheat block's logical position, the beads cheat content that was previously between the `<!-- BEGIN beads-cheat -->` and `<!-- END beads-cheat -->` markers. The marker comment lines themselves are NOT present in the deployed file. Non-cheat-block content of the deployed file is byte-equivalent to the pre-extraction version. Trailing-newline normalization: both pre- and post-extraction files are normalized to end with exactly one `\n` before comparison; the snapshot test asserts equality after this normalization. Verified by an integration test snapshot. `scripts/install.sh --dry-run` passes with the extraction in place.
10. **Generalization smoke test** — at least one fixture exercises the mechanism against an `agents/<name>.md` base file (or another non-skill base), with at least one body-section precision and one frontmatter precision.

## Design Notes — key decisions

1. **Structured YAML patches, not rules.md-mirror append.** Rejected the bead-title's "mirror rules.md pattern" framing. Append-with-`---`-separator cannot reproduce mid-document placement, conflates extension-intent with same-name-file collision, and offers no per-patch contract.
2. **Explicit `target-file` and `target-section`, not colocation.** Decouples extension location from base layout and makes intent self-documenting.
3. **Literal header text matching, not HTML anchors.** Simpler base authoring; rename-fragility accepted as the cost of avoiding a parallel anchor-declaration discipline.
4. **ATX-only header recognition + fenced-only code awareness.** A deliberate simplified subset of CommonMark. Indented code blocks are NOT recognized; base authors must avoid `#` at column 0 of indented examples. Trade-off: install.sh stays free of a full markdown parser today; can be hardened later if needed.
5. **Frontmatter as section-with-`---`-header.** All five precisions apply uniformly. Plugin content is YAML when targeting frontmatter; post-patch YAML re-parse is mandatory.
6. **Format-agnostic from day one (scope B).** Mechanism applies to any base markdown asset, not just SKILL.md.
7. **One patch per yaml file.** Cleaner failure isolation, deterministic alphabetical ordering, easier per-plugin auditability.
8. **Duplicate `target-section` is terminal.** Symmetric with "not found." Two same-header sections in a base file are usually a bug; silent first/last-match would hide it.
9. **Tool axis runs outermost; shared-before-tool within each plugin.** Each tool gets its own deterministic apply pass. Within a plugin, shared establishes the cross-tool foundation; tool layers on. Reverse ordering (tool before shared) would let tool-specific edits be overwritten by general edits — rarely useful.
10. **Code-fence-aware parsing.** SKILL.md files routinely embed shell examples (`# comment`) and markdown examples (`## Heading`). Treating these as real headers would cause spurious ambiguity errors. The fenced-only limit pairs with R4's note on indented-code avoidance.
11. **Python helper, no yq.** yq has two divergent implementations with materially different syntax. Using `python3 + PyYAML` for YAML parsing AND markdown section work keeps the helper single-language, single-dependency. The helper's CLI contract (R9) makes it independently testable.
12. **Marker comments are not preserved.** The `<!-- BEGIN beads-cheat -->` markers were extraction breadcrumbs only. The deployed file shouldn't carry artifact-of-the-extraction-process content; the deployed cheat content is what users would author from scratch with this mechanism in place.
13. **Last-write-wins is observable on plugin disable.** Because R6 ordering is plugin-alphabetical, disabling an earlier-alphabetical plugin can silently promote a later plugin's patch from shadowed to effective. Plugin authors should not assume their patch is the last word; if intent is strict layering, plugins should name yaml files to encode order explicitly, or rely on tool-scope (which always applies after shared-scope within the same plugin).
14. **Resolution happens fresh per patch.** Each extension's target-section resolves against the CURRENT state of the staged file, after all earlier patches have applied. This is a natural consequence of "later patches see earlier patches' effects" — including new headers earlier patches may have introduced.

## Open questions

None blocking implementation. All edge cases are pinned in R2–R8.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Schema validation: install.sh rejects malformed YAML, non-mapping top-level, missing required field, or unknown precision. Each error cites the fields required by R7's failure table.
2. Target resolution: install.sh rejects missing target-file, target-section with zero or ≥2 header matches, OR target-section: frontmatter against a file lacking a leading frontmatter block. Each error cites per R7.
3. Body-section precisions: all five precisions produce expected diffs against fixtures. Coverage includes trailing blank lines, nested sub-sections at depth D+1, fenced code blocks containing # (not treated as headers), and R4 append worked examples verified byte-exact.
4. Frontmatter precisions: all five precisions work on target-section: frontmatter; replace preserves both --- delimiters; resulting YAML re-parses cleanly. Missing-frontmatter cases rejected per R5/R7.
5. Ordering: extensions across (tool × plugin-alphabetical × scope-shared-before-tool × filename-alphabetical) produce expected order against the same target; fixture includes a case where ordering changes the result.
6. Composition with introduced headers: extension whose content introduces an ATX header that a later extension targets resolves correctly. Fixture covers both 'now-resolvable' (0→1 matches) and 'now-ambiguous' (1→≥2, terminal) cases.
7. Tool-scope isolation: .claude/extensions/ patch appears in Claude deployed output only and NOT in Codex; .agents/extensions/ patch appears in both.
8. Extraction performed: merge-and-cleanup skill's inline beads cheat block extracted to src/plugins/beads/.agents/extensions/; base SKILL.md no longer contains inline block OR marker comments.
9. Extraction round-trip equivalence: deployed cheat content matches pre-extraction between-marker content at same logical position; marker lines absent; non-cheat-block content byte-equivalent (modulo trailing newline). Integration snapshot test. scripts/install.sh --dry-run passes.
10. Generalization smoke test: ≥1 fixture exercises mechanism on a non-SKILL.md base (e.g., agents/<name>.md), with at least one body-section and one frontmatter precision.
notes: Deferred: requirements translated into w1qls.6.5 (F.5 — plugin-to-base-asset extension via YAML patches, Python). Implement against the Python installer package, not install.sh. The Bash+Python-helper hybrid in this spec is obsolete once w1qls lands.

title: Skill-extension mechanism: structured YAML patches, not rules.md-style append
notes:
Bead title proposed mirroring the rules.md append-with-separator pattern for SKILL.md. Brainstorm rejected that framing: append-only cannot reproduce mid-document placement (the inline beads cheat block sits mid-document per acmh.5 §R2 step 6), conflates extension-intent with same-name-file collision, and offers no per-patch declarative contract.

Adopted: structured YAML patch manifests at <plugin>/.agents/extensions/ and <plugin>/.<tool>/extensions/. Each yaml declares target-file (staging-root-relative), target-section (literal header text OR keyword 'frontmatter'), precision (5 verbs), content. Format-agnostic — applies to any base markdown asset.

Sub-decisions resolved in the spec:
- Target match: literal header text (not anchors). Rename-fragility accepted.
- Target file: explicit field, not colocation. Decouples extension location from base layout.
- Frontmatter: treated as section-with-'---'-header. All 5 precisions apply uniformly.
- Scope: generalize to any base markdown (not SKILL.md only). Same engineering cost.
- Granularity: one patch per yaml file. Deterministic ordering, isolated failures.
- Failure modes: target missing or ambiguous = terminal. No partial application.

See agents-config-phzj.3 notes for the full spec.

title: [Impl] merge-and-cleanup skill, CLIs, install.sh enhancement, formula refactors
description:
# Spec: Cross-cutting formulas-as-skills review (acmh.5)

## Summary
Survey of all 5 bead formulas for skill-extraction candidates produces ONE new skill — `merge-and-cleanup` — designed as a beads-agnostic, ad-hoc-invokable skill backed by three CLI helpers. All other audit-flagged "extraction" candidates resolve to either existing skill invocations, beads-specific orchestration that stays inline in formulas, or pure-mechanics CLI helpers (tracked by acmh.12). One discovered-from follow-up bead filed: agents-config-phzj.3 (install.sh skill-extension mechanism).

## Findings (spike outcome)

**Q (bead)**: For each formula, what should be translated into one or more skills?

**A**: One new skill (`merge-and-cleanup`), three CLI helpers (gh-merge mechanics, beads close-walk wrapper, PR-readiness checker), four invocation tiers. All other formulas already invoke the right skills today.

### Per-formula verdict

| Formula | Skill-extraction candidates evaluated | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| `brainstorm-bead` | finalize formula-selection + handoff logic — *evaluated, rejected* | **Skip** — beads-specific orchestration, stays inline |
| `implement-feature` | adds terminal merge-step that invokes merge-and-cleanup skill (Tier C) | **No new skills** — invokes existing skill at end |
| `fix-bug` | adds terminal merge-step that invokes merge-and-cleanup skill (Tier C) | **No new skills** — invokes existing skill at end |
| `docs-only` | none beyond existing | **No new skills** |
| **`merge-and-cleanup`** | THE skill (formula collapses to Tier-D wisp wrapping it) | **DESIGN** below |

## Background / Motivation
Tier-2 audit finding il69 surfaced that several formula steps mix mechanical bash with agent judgment. The user named `merge-and-cleanup` as the canonical candidate for skill-extraction ("often wished as ad-hoc invokable skill"). acmh.5 was split out of acmh.4 (formulas/ cleanups) so the merge-and-cleanup-specific findings (F8, F9, F10, F15) could be resolved by skill-extraction rather than formula prose edits.

Strategic intent (per discuss step):
- Agent should be minimally involved in merge-and-cleanup beyond orchestration + troubleshooting.
- Mechanics live in deterministic CLI helpers; skill is a thin agent-facing wrapper.
- Skill is beads-unaware at runtime (consumers without beads must work end-to-end). Beads-specific extension carried in an inline cheat block delimited by HTML comments, to be extracted to a plugin overlay file once agents-config-phzj.3 ships.

## Requirements

### R2 — merge-and-cleanup skill design

**Location**: `src/user/.agents/skills/merge-and-cleanup/SKILL.md` (shared, beads-agnostic at runtime).

**Skill body (prose contract)**:
1. **Detect invocation context**: open PR number (required) and bead/molecule context observed from prior conversation/active formula (optional — informs cheat block activation).
2. **Invoke PR-readiness check** via `gh-pr-readiness-check.sh --pr <N> --json` (B3 below). Helper returns structured findings categorized as:
   - `bot_review_pending` — a bot reviewer (Copilot, Codex, etc.) submitted a review on a stale commit but not on the current HEAD.
   - `unresolved_threads` — GraphQL `pullRequestThread.isResolved == false` AND last comment author ≠ PR author (genuine unresponded actionable feedback). Each entry may carry an advisory `escalation_hint: true` annotation if its last comment matches the configured escalation-phrase regex (advisory only — does NOT gate readiness).
   - `pending_review_request` — `reviewRequests` non-empty (CODEOWNERS / required reviewer outstanding).
   - `review_decision_block` — `reviewDecision` ∈ {REVIEW_REQUIRED, CHANGES_REQUESTED}.
3. **Triage readiness findings**: any non-empty signal → present findings to user, ask for explicit override-or-wait. NO findings → proceed. Override response (verbatim user text + timestamp) is captured in the audit trail per D9. **Note on `bot_review_pending` UX**: this signal will fire on most PRs whose bots reviewed an early commit and then the PR received further pushes (GitHub exposes no native "review complete vs. in-flight" marker — see Feasibility Annex). Expect override-with-explanation to be a routine flow for this signal specifically; the skill MUST present `bot_review_pending` findings with a one-line context blurb explaining the staleness heuristic so the user can make an informed override decision.
4. **Verify explicit merge authorization** — two paths. **Conveyance is prose-based, not a literal CLI flag**: SKILL.md is read by an agent, not invoked as a binary, so "passes through" means the captured phrase lives in conversation context (Tier A/B) or in the prior molecule step's notes (Tier C/D) that the agent reading the skill prose can directly observe.
   - **Pre-captured auth (Tier C/D and any caller that captured "ship it"-class phrase before invoking the skill)**: prior formula/wisp step captures the phrase in its OWN user prompt and writes it to that step's notes (e.g., `bd update <step-bead-id> --append-notes "auth-phrase: <verbatim>"`). The skill body instructs the executing agent to read those notes and validate the phrase against R8's **authoritative phrase set** using R8's comparator. Match → proceed. Mismatch → HALT and re-prompt the user via path B below; on a valid re-prompt, the prior step's notes are updated via `bd update --append-notes "auth-phrase-corrected: <new-verbatim>"` so the audit trail records the actual phrase used. (Note: prior-step responsibility for capture-and-validate per R6 should prevent this re-prompt path in practice — see D5.)
   - **No pre-captured auth (Tier A/B direct invocation)**: skill prompts the user for explicit authorization in the current conversation turn, validates against the authoritative phrase set using R8's comparator.
   In both paths, NO valid authorization → HALT. Trust-but-verify is non-negotiable.
5. **Invoke `gh-pr-merge-and-cleanup.sh --pr <N> [--worktree <path>] [--branch <name>] [--mode squash|merge|rebase]`** (B1) to perform PR merge + branch delete + worktree teardown. Skill reads B1's JSON output to determine outcome (see B1 exit-code contract in R3); B1 exit code 0 always means "script ran" — outcome lives in JSON.
6. **Inline beads-cheat block** (extract-later): "if the current work is tracked by a bead or molecule step bead, also run `bd-merge-cleanup.sh --bead-id <id>` (B2) to perform the beads close-walk and merge-gate-child detection." Block delimited by `<!-- BEGIN beads-cheat — extract via agents-config-phzj.3 -->` / `<!-- END beads-cheat -->`. Cheat-block invariants in R4. **Conditional execution**: Tier-A invocations skip this block; Tier-B/C/D activate it.
7. **Report outcome**: PR URL, merge SHA, branch/worktree status, beads context status (if cheat block activated), any override decisions captured in step 3.

**Four invocation tiers** all hit the same skill — they differ only by whether bead/molecule context is observable to the agent. **All four tiers run the same PR-readiness check in step 2** — Tier A is not exempt.
- **Tier A (untracked PR)**: open PR with no bead/molecule context; skill runs end-to-end without invoking the cheat block; skill prompts directly for authorization (R2 step 4 second path).
- **Tier B (bead-tracked, no wisp)**: agent observes the bead context from conversation; cheat block activates; auth flow follows whichever path applies (typically Tier-A second path because there's no formula step capturing the phrase).
- **Tier C (mid-formula fast-path; DEFAULT for new work)**: implement-feature / fix-bug formula adds a terminal merge step. That step captures the user's authorization phrase in its OWN prompt, then invokes the skill with `--authorization "<phrase>"`. Skill validates without re-prompting (R2 step 4 first path). Bead context = the active molecule's source bead.
- **Tier D (merge-and-cleanup wisp; retroactive / cross-formula path)**: the existing `merge-and-cleanup` formula refactored to a two-step thin wisp (R6). Survives for cases where Tier C wasn't used (work merged via separate `bd mol pour merge-and-cleanup`).

### R3 — Three CLI helpers (ship together with the skill in the Y bead)

All three helpers share an **exit-code contract**: exit 0 means "script ran to a defined outcome" (success OR documented partial-state OR documented refusal); the JSON output carries the actual outcome via dedicated fields. Non-zero exit is reserved for **usage errors** (bad args, missing required dependency, gh not authenticated) — never for partial-success states. This keeps `set -e` callers (the skill) from short-circuiting before they read the JSON.

**B1: `gh-pr-merge-and-cleanup.sh`** — location: `src/user/.agents/scripts/`, deployed to `~/.agents/scripts/` by install.sh (see R10 — install.sh enhancement in Y scope).
- Args: `--pr <N>` (required), `--worktree <path>` (optional), `--branch <name>` (optional), `--mode squash|merge|rebase` (optional; default `squash`).
- Behavior: `gh pr merge --<mode> --delete-branch <N>`; if `--worktree` given, attempt `git worktree remove --force` after merge succeeds.
- Output: JSON `{ "merge": "ok|failed", "merge_sha": "<sha>|null", "branch": "deleted|preserved|n/a", "worktree": "removed|failed|n/a", "error": "<msg>|null" }`.
- Beads-unaware.

**B2: `bd-merge-cleanup.sh`** — location: `src/plugins/beads/.beads/scripts/`, deployed to `~/.beads/scripts/` via existing plugin scripts mechanism.
- Args: `--bead-id <id>` (required), `--reason <text>` (optional; default `"Merged via merge-and-cleanup skill"`).
- Behavior — explicit phase list (preserves existing formula's 3-phase ordering). **B2 is purely deterministic — it does NOT execute HEP**; on data-anomaly outcomes, it emits a structured outcome and exits 0, letting the skill (which has conversational context, molecule-ID, and worktree path) decide whether to escalate via HEP.
  1. **Detect merge-gate child** of `<bead-id>` via the F10 logic. If exactly one → proceed to phase 2. If zero → emit `{ "outcome": "no_merge_gate_child", ... }` and exit 0. If two or more → emit `{ "outcome": "multiple_merge_gate_children", "merge_gate_candidates": ["<id1>", "<id2>", ...] }` and exit 0. **Skill is responsible for invoking HEP on these data-anomaly outcomes** (the skill has the molecule context HEP requires).
  2. **Close merge-gate child** with `bd close <merge-gate-id> --reason "<reason> (merge-gate)"`.
  3. **Close source bead** with `bd close <bead-id> --reason "<reason>"`.
  4. **Invoke** `~/.beads/scripts/bd-close-walk.sh --bead-id <bead-id>` to walk ancestor epics (closes any whose remaining children are all closed). **WRAPS, does not reimplement** `bd-close-walk.sh`.
- Output: JSON `{ "outcome": "ok|no_merge_gate_child|multiple_merge_gate_children|partial", "merge_gate_child": "<id>|null", "merge_gate_candidates": ["<id>", ...], "closed": ["<id>", ...], "walked": <N>, "error": "<msg>|null" }`.
- Beads-specific.

**B3: `gh-pr-readiness-check.sh`** — location: `src/user/.agents/scripts/`, deployed to `~/.agents/scripts/` (see R10).
- Args: `--pr <N>` (required), `--json` (optional; default true), `--bot-authors <comma-list>` (optional; default `"copilot-pull-request-reviewer,chatgpt-codex-connector"`), `--escalation-phrases <regex-pattern>` (optional override of the default escalation-phrase pattern for `escalation_hint` annotation; value MUST be a single POSIX-extended regex — commas are NOT separators; see Feasibility Annex for default).
- Behavior: queries gh CLI / GraphQL for the four readiness signals enumerated in R2 step 2 (see Feasibility Annex below for exact probes). `escalation_hint` annotations are computed on `unresolved_threads` entries via regex match; they are NOT a standalone gating signal.
- Output: JSON `{ "ready": true|false, "signals": { "bot_review_pending": [...], "unresolved_threads": [{ "id": "...", "path": "...", "line": ..., "last_comment_author": "...", "escalation_hint": true|false }, ...], "pending_review_request": [...], "review_decision_block": "<state>|null" } }`. `ready=true` iff all four signal arrays/values are empty/null. (`escalation_hint` is advisory only.)
- Beads-unaware.

### R4 — Inline beads-cheat block invariants
The skill's beads section is bracketed by HTML comments naming agents-config-phzj.3 as the bead that will eventually extract it. Convention:
- **Single contiguous region** between the BEGIN/END markers.
- **Self-contained** — readable as standalone prose without referring to surrounding sections.
- **Deletable without breaking surrounding prose** — the base skill flow MUST remain coherent if the cheat block is removed in entirety. Tier-A users effectively see this scenario at runtime.
- **No other content between BEGIN/END** — no nested conditionals, no other plugin's extension content. One cheat block per plugin.
- **Extraction direction-of-travel** (not a hard guarantee): the cheat block is structured to make extraction by phzj.3 mechanically trivial. If phzj.3's eventual design requires shape changes (e.g., per-section append markers, YAML overlay, templating shim), the adjustments will be captured at phzj.3's spec time as a discovered-from edge back to acmh.5. This spec does not bind a not-yet-brainstormed sibling to a verbatim-move commitment.

### R5 — Follow-up bead production (finalize stamps `produced-bead-<Y>`)
Exactly ONE Y bead, covering: the skill, three CLI helpers, install.sh enhancement (R10), Tier-C terminal merge steps in `implement-feature.formula.toml` + `fix-bug.formula.toml`, Tier-D wisp refactor of `merge-and-cleanup.formula.toml`, and delivery.md auth-phrase reference rewrite (R8) — all as one cohesive impl unit. Parent: agents-config-abn9 (M1). Discovered-from: acmh.5. The brainstorm-bead finalize step files it and stamps the produced-from edge.

Note on scope: Y is wider than initially scoped due to user direction (keep Tier C in scope; expand to update install.sh). If Y's implementer surfaces blast-radius concerns, they may split portions into a child bead. Decision deferred to Y bead.

### R6 — Tier-D wisp shape (current merge-and-cleanup formula refactor)
The existing `merge-and-cleanup.formula.toml` is refactored to a two-step wisp:
- **Step 1 — `preflight`**: validates `pr` and `bead-id` vars; performs claim-walk on the source bead (marks in_progress + ancestors); **captures the user's authorization phrase with R8 validation in-step** — i.e., this step prompts, validates against R8's phrase set using R8's comparator, and re-prompts until valid before committing the validated phrase to the step's notes (`bd update <step-bead-id> --append-notes "auth-phrase: <verbatim>"`). Step 2 can then trust the notes.
- **Step 2 — `invoke-skill`**: invokes the `merge-and-cleanup` skill with observable bead context. Skill prose (R2 step 4 first path) reads the validated auth phrase from step 1's notes. Skill executes R2's flow (steps 1–7), with step 6 (cheat block) activating because bead context is present.

Vars retained: `pr`, `bead-id`. Vars dropped: `title-slug` (only needed for step-bead naming, irrelevant in a 2-step wisp).

**Backward compatibility**: existing molecule instances created from the current 7-step formula retain their vars (including `title-slug`) in their persistent state — no migration required. Only new pours/wisps after Y lands use the slimmer schema.

The existing formula's steps `verify-completion-gate-evidence`, `check-pr-comments`, `authorize-merge`, `merge`, `cleanup`, `audit` collapse into the skill body (R2). F8 (file header purpose statement) still applies to the slimmer file header in acmh.4's scope; F9 disappears (no separate authorize-merge step survives); F10 is implemented by B2; F15 disappears (delegate-to-wait-for-pr-comments wording superseded by lighter PR-readiness check living in B3).

### R7 — Hook-convention idea bound to AC #6 (and phzj.3)
Future extensibility (out of Y scope, deferred to phzj.3 brainstorm): pre-/post-merge hook script directory convention under `merge-and-cleanup/scripts/extensions/`. Main `gh-pr-merge-and-cleanup.sh` would auto-discover and invoke matching hooks at corresponding hook points, with a contract for status/error reporting, ordering, and stdout isolation.

This requirement is satisfied by acceptance criterion #6 — a comment with full hook-convention exploration notes posted on phzj.3 during this spike's discuss step.

### R8 — Auth-phrase source of truth, comparator, and reference mechanism
The skill is the **authoritative source** for the merge-authorization phrase set. The set is enumerated explicitly in SKILL.md R2 step 4 prose.

**Initial phrase set**: `"ship it"`, `"go ahead and merge"`, `"merge it"`, `"merge that"`, `"approved, merge"`. Y bead's implementer adjusts only with explicit user signoff.

**Comparator (deterministic)**: case-insensitive substring match against the **normalized user response**, where normalization is: lowercase, trim leading/trailing whitespace, collapse internal whitespace runs to single spaces. A response matches if at least one phrase in R8's set is found as a contained substring of the normalized response. Examples:
- `"yeah, go ahead and merge it now"` → matches (`"go ahead and merge"` is a substring).
- `"ship it!"` → matches after normalization (trailing `!` is not part of the phrase but the substring still matches).
- `"yeah, go"` → no match (no enumerated phrase is contained).
- `"GO AHEAD AND MERGE"` → matches (case-insensitive normalization).

**Reference mechanism (interim, picks the cheap-and-clear option)**: Y bead edits `src/user/.claude/rules/delivery.md` to remove its current enumerated phrase list and replace with a prose pointer naming the `merge-and-cleanup` skill (R2 step 4) as the authoritative source. **Wording flexibility**: Y's implementer may adjust the pointer wording for tone; the contract is (a) no enumerated phrases remain in delivery.md, (b) the pointer names the merge-and-cleanup skill as authoritative, (c) human readers can follow the pointer to find the actual phrase set.

**Future-state (out of Y scope, captured as discovered-from from Y)**: factor the phrase set into a shared file consumed by both surfaces (e.g., `~/.agents/auth-phrases.txt`) so the reference is mechanical rather than prose-only. Y's implementer files a discovered-from bead capturing this idea.

### R9 — acmh.4 ripple (concrete owner: this spike's finalize step)
On finalize, do BOTH:
1. **Post a comment** on agents-config-acmh.4 summarizing the ripple.
2. **Edit acmh.4's description** to:
   - Strike F9 from in-scope (no separate authorize-merge step survives in the Tier-D wisp).
   - Strike F10 from in-scope (absorbed by B2 in Y).
   - Strike F15 from in-scope (superseded by the skill's lighter PR-readiness check; not absorbed).
   - Keep F8 in-scope (slimmer file header still needs the purpose-statement rewrite).

acmh.4 unblocks once Y lands.

### R10 — install.sh enhancement (in Y scope per user direction on C1)
Y bead expands `scripts/install.sh` to deploy shared agent scripts:
- New install path: `src/user/.agents/scripts/*` → `~/.agents/scripts/` (mirror existing `src/plugins/<plugin>/.beads/scripts/` → `~/.beads/scripts/` pattern at install.sh lines 1018–1097).
- Helpers B1 (`gh-pr-merge-and-cleanup.sh`) and B3 (`gh-pr-readiness-check.sh`) ship via this path so they're available regardless of which plugins are enabled (preserves Tier-A "no beads required" intent).
- B2 (`bd-merge-cleanup.sh`) continues to ship via the existing beads-plugin scripts mechanism (only deployed when beads plugin enabled — correct, because B2 is beads-specific).
- **Prune behavior**: shared scripts MIRROR the existing beads-scripts no-prune behavior (`--prune` does NOT remove scripts that are no longer in source). This is intentional and consistent — scripts are rare, hand-managed, and a user removing one from source should not silently lose their installed copy. Y bead documents this in install.sh prose alongside the new install path.

This bundling is by user direction (acmh.5 discuss): preferable to opening yet another install-mechanism sibling bead and hard-blocking Y on it.

## Out of Scope (revised)
- **Implementation** of the skill, CLIs, formula edits, install.sh enhancement, delivery.md rewrite (Y bead's job — but ALL of these are now in Y's scope).
- **install.sh skill-extension injection mechanism for SKILL.md** (agents-config-phzj.3 — itself needs future brainstorm before impl). Distinct from R10's scripts-deploy path which IS in Y.
- **Other formula-level audit findings** (acmh.4's F8 prose edit; acmh.12's CLI helper extractions — except B2 which Y absorbs because it implements F10).
- **Hook-convention design** for pre-/post-merge scripts (deferred to phzj.3 brainstorm).
- **brainstorm-bead, docs-only** structural restructuring (no new skills surfaced; existing skill invocations already cover them).
- **Shared auth-phrases file mechanism** (R8 future-state — Y files a discovered-from bead).

NOTE: implement-feature and fix-bug **ARE in scope** per Tier C — Y bead adds a terminal merge step to both. This replaces any earlier "Out of Scope §4" wording about not restructuring those formulas.

## Acceptance Criteria (inlined in spec; bead acceptance field carries the same list)

1. Per-formula verdict produced for all 5 formulas. implement-feature and fix-bug confirmed as Tier-C consumers, not new-skill sources.
2. merge-and-cleanup skill design specified: beads-agnostic at runtime SKILL.md body, FOUR invocation tiers (all running PR-readiness check), pre-captured-auth path for Tier-C/D + direct-prompt path for Tier-A/B, code-backed PR-readiness check with override-or-wait UX on findings, inline beads-cheat block with R4 invariants delimited by HTML comments naming phzj.3.
3. Three supporting CLI helpers specified with args + behavior + JSON output + exit-code contract (exit 0 always means "script ran"; outcome via JSON; non-zero only for usage errors).

## Design Notes / Decisions Made
- **D1 (skill-vs-formula intent)**: skill is the canonical implementation; beads workflow USES the skill, not vice versa.
- **D2 (beads-agnostic at runtime, not at file content)**: skill is shared (`src/user/.agents/skills/`), not plugin-scoped. Consumers without beads run it end-to-end with no beads-side calls executed. The base SKILL.md *does* carry a clearly-bracketed beads-extension block in its file content (until phzj.3 ships), but Tier-A users observe and execute the file as if the block were absent.
- **D3 (one Y bead, blast-radius known)**: skill + three CLIs + install.sh enhancement + two formula step-additions + one formula refactor + delivery.md edit ships as one Y. Y implementer may split if they hit limits.
- **D4 (cheat block now, mechanism later)**: install.sh has no plugin → SKILL.md append mechanism today. Inline beads section in base SKILL.md is pragmatic interim; phzj.3 captures the formal-mechanism work. R4 invariants govern the cheat block's shape; R4's last bullet softens "MUST move verbatim" to "structured for trivial extraction; phzj.3 may adjust at its spec time."
- **D5 (auth gate non-negotiable, single auth event per merge)**: skill ALWAYS validates against its authoritative phrase set (R8). Tier C/D pre-capture the phrase and pass it through so the user authorizes ONCE per merge — not once at the formula step AND once at the skill. Tier A/B direct invocations prompt the user.
- **D6 (formula survives as Tier D wisp)**: `merge-and-cleanup.formula.toml` refactored to two-step wisp (R6). NOT deleted — preserves the molecule audit trail.
- **D7 (PR-readiness vs. wait-for-pr-comments — composable, not overlapping)**:
  - `wait-for-pr-comments` OWNS the **classify-fix-push-reply-resolve** cycle. Mutates: writes fixes, pushes commits, posts replies, resolves threads.
  - `merge-and-cleanup`'s readiness check OWNS the **merge-time go/no-go signal**, NOT the resolution of any comment. Read-only against the PR surface; emits structured findings.
  - Composable workflow: agent runs wait-for-pr-comments first (if any feedback exists); then merge-and-cleanup's readiness check confirms residual state is mergeable. Readiness check **assumes** wait-for-pr-comments has already run if it was needed.
- **D8 (Tier C vs Tier D canonicality)**: Tier C is the DEFAULT path for new work tracked via implement-feature/fix-bug. Tier D survives for retroactive merge (work whose original formula didn't include a terminal merge step) and for ad-hoc / cross-formula bead-tracked merges. Both paths exist intentionally.
- **D9 (audit trail medium and format)**: For Tier B/C/D, the skill appends a structured audit block to the source bead's notes via `bd update <bead-id> --append-notes` at three points: after readiness-check completes, after any override decision, and after the final merge outcome. **Format**: each append is a marker line `[merge-and-cleanup audit @<ISO8601-timestamp>]` followed by `key: value` lines, e.g.:
  ```
  [merge-and-cleanup audit @2026-05-14T17:23:00Z]
  phase: readiness-check
  pr: 123
  ready: false
  signals: bot_review_pending=[copilot:14f3ccd3] unresolved_threads=[] pending_review_request=[] review_decision_block=null
  ```
  Subsequent appends (override, merge-outcome) follow the same pattern. For Tier B (bead-tracked, no wisp) the skill reads `<bead-id>` from observable bead context in the conversation and writes directly to that bead's notes — no intermediary mechanism. For Tier A (no bead), the conversation transcript IS the audit trail — no extra persistence required.
- **D10 (exit-code contract, R3 paragraph)**: see R3's leading paragraph. Always exit 0 unless the script literally couldn't run; outcome flows through JSON. Keeps `set -e` callers safe and JSON-first.

## Feasibility Annex — gh probes for PR-readiness check

Validated against real example: agents-config PR #67 (HEP rollout) — 30 reviews from Copilot, Codex, and the PR owner; 30 inline review comments; multiple bot review submissions across the PR's lifetime.

GitHub's REST/GraphQL API exposes the following signals; B3 composes these into the structured output specified in R3.

| Readiness signal | Primary probe | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `bot_review_pending` (bot reviewed older commit, not HEAD) | `gh pr view <N> --json reviews,headRefOid` then jq filter for reviews whose `commit.oid` ≠ `headRefOid` AND author ∈ known bot list (`copilot-pull-request-reviewer`, `chatgpt-codex-connector`). | Bot list is configurable in B3 via `--bot-authors` arg (default carries the two above). Robust against PR #67's observed pattern: each bot reviewed the commit-of-the-moment at the time of review, not a single sustained "running" review. |
| `unresolved_threads` (actionable comments with no author resolution) | `gh api graphql -f query='{repository(owner:OWN,name:REP){pullRequest(number:N){reviewThreads(first:100){nodes{isResolved,path,line,comments(first:50){nodes{author{login},body,createdAt}}}}}}}'` then filter `isResolved == false` AND last-comment author ≠ PR author. Per-entry, compute `escalation_hint` as advisory annotation by regex-matching last comment body. | GraphQL `isResolved` is the canonical signal. REST `/pulls/N/comments` does not expose thread-resolution state; only GraphQL does. |
| `pending_review_request` (CODEOWNERS or required reviewer unmet) | `gh pr view <N> --json reviewRequests` — non-empty list means at least one requested reviewer has not submitted. | reviewRequests gets cleared when a review is submitted; persistent value = genuine pending. |
| `review_decision_block` (overall PR review-state) | `gh pr view <N> --json reviewDecision` — value ∈ {APPROVED, REVIEW_REQUIRED, CHANGES_REQUESTED}. | Treat REVIEW_REQUIRED and CHANGES_REQUESTED as hard-stop signals. APPROVED clears the signal. |
| `escalation_hint` (advisory, NOT a separate signal) | Same GraphQL as `unresolved_threads`. Each unresolved-thread entry carries `escalation_hint: true` iff its last comment matches the default phrase regex `/(escalat(ed|ion)|deferred (to|until)|blocked on human|needs (design|product) input)/i`. | Demoted from a standalone gating signal to an advisory annotation. Default regex is intentionally narrower than the prior "deferred" catch-all to reduce false positives like "I deferred the typo fix." Configurable via B3's `--escalation-phrases` arg. |

**Key API observations from PR #67:**
- GitHub does **NOT** expose a native "review in progress" state for bots. Bots indicate "reviewing now" by eventually submitting a review with `state == COMMENTED`. The "is the bot currently looking?" question is undecidable from the API alone. Our `bot_review_pending` heuristic (reviewed an older commit but not HEAD) is the closest proxy.
- Both Copilot and Codex use `state == COMMENTED` (never APPROVED / CHANGES_REQUESTED). `reviewDecision` is unaffected by bot reviews unless the bot is in CODEOWNERS.
- PR #67 carried 3 separate Copilot review submissions and 3 separate Codex review submissions across the PR's lifetime. Cross-commit multiplicity confirmed; per-(author, commit) multiplicity not specifically validated by this annex — Y bead's implementer should sanity-check on a small sample whether bots ever submit two reviews on the same commit and adjust B3's dedupe rule (default: latest review per (author, commit) wins) if needed.
- Top-level PR comments (`/issues/N/comments`) were empty in PR #67; all bot output came through the review surface (`/pulls/N/reviews`). B3 focuses on the review surface.

## Open Questions
None blocking. Y-bead first design tasks:
1. **Instrumentation**: should the skill emit a lightweight log line (or PR comment, or bead-note tag) so we can observe adoption rate, false-positive readiness signals, and override patterns? Y bead's implementer decides; the D9 audit blocks already give per-invocation evidence — instrumentation question is whether to aggregate.
2. **`bot_review_pending` future enhancement**: M4 in cycle 2 review surfaced that the stale-bot signal will fire chronically. Initial implementation accepts this and documents the override UX (per R2 step 3). Y bead's implementer may add a time-window cap (e.g., "bot reviews older than 14 days OR more than 3 commits ago are treated as completed-and-stale") as a follow-up improvement; not blocking initial Y completion.
